EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a warm and moist airmass in place over the region, thunderstorms developed along lake breezes during the afternoon and evening.  The persistent storms were prolific hail producers���not only in size but quantity. At times it was reported the hail completely covered the ground. The largest stones approached two inches, however most of the hail exceeded one-inch in diameter.  Hail was reported near Lake Bluff, Port Bay, Red Creek, Victory, Martville, West Monroe and Hastings, to name a few. While most of the severe reports were of large hail, a few storms did produce damaging winds with trees and wires reported down near Ira, Victory, Phoenix, Wellsville, Adams Center and Batavia.

The region remained in a warm and humid air mass as an upper level ridge remained just south and east across the western Atlantic Ocean. At the same time, a weak upper level disturbance passed by to the north across southern Canada.  This upper level disturbance allowed showers and thunderstorms to develop across the region during the afternoon and early evening hours of Monday, June 24th.  With a moist and unstable atmosphere in place, some of these storms became severe, with large hail and damaging wind gusts.  Thunderstorms dissipated by later in the evening, as the disturbance moved east of the region and the atmosphere stabilized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several spotters reported half dollar to golfball size hail during a thunderstorm in Wyantskill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm developed ahead of an approaching cold front. Initially over northwest Pennsylvania, the storm intensified as it moved north into Allegany County. The thunderstorm not only produced one-and-one-half in hail but damaging winds that downed trees and tree limbs. One was damaged when a tree fell on it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although this snow event occurred simultaneously with the Long Island-Southern New England Blizzard, the main culprit in northern New York was from a relatively strong, but slowly dissipating storm from the Great Lakes. ||Snow began to fall across northern New York after midnight on February 8th and continued for much of the day, with the heaviest occurring during the morning hours. Storm total snowfall ranged from 6 to 12 inches with localized higher amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight to fourteen inches of snow well across St. Lawrence county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The fourth lake effect event of the season was a combination of light synoptic snows followed lake effect snows.  Off Lake Erie, snow fell through the morning and afternoon hours of the 18th before migrating northward through the evening hours and dissipating. The lake effect band of snow off Lake Ontario intensified through the late morning and afternoon hours of the 18th while impacting the Tug Hill region. This band too also shifted northward overnight and weakened. Snowfall rates peaked at two inches per hour. Within the higher snowfall rates, thunder snow occurred. Gusty winds and the timing of early morning did bring accidents on area roads slowing the morning commute. Specific amounts reported included:  12 inches at West Falls and Highmarket; 11 inches as Boston and Lacona; 10 inches at Dunkirk and 8 inches as Constableville.

A surface front stalled across the region acted as a pathway for periods of heavy precipitation. South of the front, rain fell however to the north the precipitation fell as freezing rain. Ice coated trees, power lines and all exposed surfaces. Across the Niagara Frontier, ice accumulations of a half- to one-inch were reported. Across the north country, where the freezing rain persisted the longest, the ice accumulations ranged from one to two inches. The weight of the heavy ice brought down trees and power lines. In some cases, trees fell on homes, buildings and automobiles. Tens of thousands were left without power. New York State issued an Emergency Declaration for Jefferson and Lewis counties���the hardest hit of the region.

A weak mid-atmospheric disturbance moving across a stationary surface boundary within an unstable, tropical air mass led to numerous showers and thunderstorms with torrential, flooding rains across portions of northeast New York. Rainfall rates of 2 inches per hour were observed and detected by Dual-Pol radar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding closed and damaged roads throughout Beekmantown and Peru in Clinton County, including Route 22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along various boundaries within a warm and moist air mass over the region. Storms first developed across the Finger Lakes midday along the Lake Ontario lake breeze and then in the mid afternoon along the Lake Erie lake breeze boundary as it pushed inland across the Southern Tier. The strongest storms produced one to two inch hailstones.  Specific hail reports were received from Stanley, Geneva, Newark, Walworth, Marion, and Brockport. In several of those locations, automobiles were damage by the hail stones.  Later in the afternoon thunderstorms rolled across the North Country with downed trees and power poles reported.

A warm and humid air mass was in place across the region on the evening on Saturday, June 1st into the day on Sunday, June 2nd.  Daytime temperatures reached well into the 80s across much of the area. A cold front and associated pre-frontal surface trough was moving towards the area over the weekend. With plenty of moisture and instability in place, showers and thunderstorms developed across the region. These thunderstorms were aided by very strong winds aloft and a few storms became severe across eastern New York, producing wind damage and large hail. The thunderstorms weakened by evening as the cold front moved across the area, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple large trees were reported down as a result of thunderstorm winds in Cambridge.  A rose trellis was also snapped as well.

The first major lake effect event of the 2013-2014 seasoned occurred over the areas east of Lakes Erie and Ontario beginning Tuesday, December 10.  The long-lived event started with lake effect developing during Tuesday morning and moving north and south across areas east of the lakes several times over the two-day event.|Off Lake Erie, a band of lake effect snow developed over the Chautauqua Ridge where as much as a half foot fell by afternoon. The band lifted into the Buffalo Metro area then settled back to the south later in the evening. During Wednesday, the band became quite intense with an extremely sharp edge on its northern boundary, cutting across Hamburg, East Aurora, and Elma. Snowfall rates reached three to five inches an hour in the most intense portion of the band.  The New York State Thruway was closed for several hours between Dunkirk and Hamburg. The band shifted south by evening and continued to snow throughout the night in the Boston Hills. Specific snowfall reports included:  35 inches at Colden, 26 inches at Wales and Boston, 15 inches at Dunkirk, and 8 inches at Stafford. |Off Lake Ontario, a similar story played out with an east-west oriented band developing and quickly Tuesday morning, dropping a half foot over upslope regions of the Tug Hill by Tuesday afternoon. The band intensified significantly Tuesday night, remaining in place with little movement through Wednesday night. Snowfall amounts of three to four feet fell in a 24- to 36-hour period.  Specific snowfall reports included:  44 inches at Redfield and Constableville, 20 inches at Bennetts Bridge, 18 inches at Mannsville and 16 inches at Lowville.

The second lake effect event of the season occurred on the heels of the first.|Off Lake Erie, lake effect snow lifted north across the Buffalo metro area on Thursday December 12 during the rush hour commute.  The Buffalo Airport received a quick 6 for this event while areas to the north and west received up to a foot before the band moved south generally remaining in place through Friday.  The hardest hit areas included northwest Cattaraugus, northern Chautauqua, and southern and central Erie Counties.  Specific snowfall amounts included: 27 inches at Perrysburg, 23 inches at Glenwood, 22 inches at Warsaw, 14 inches at Dunkirk and 8 inches at Marilla.|Off Lake Ontario, a strong lake band developed and intensified just north of Watertown during the morning of 12th. Later in the afternoon, the band moved south back over the Tug Hill region and remained in place through daybreak Friday morning. Finally the band dropped south across Oswego county and dissipated during the morning as an arctic dry airmass settled into the region. The heaviest hit areas received four to six feet of snow. Specific reports included:  23 inches at Copenhagen, 22 inches at Redfield, 21 inches at Highmarket, and 17 inches at Lowville.

A persistent upper level trough over eastern Canada kept a cold westerly flow over the Great Lakes. The event started Sunday afternoon (December 15th) to the east of Lake Erie during the afternoon hours. The snow fell at up to three inches per hour with near zero visibility in a 15-mile wide band from Hamburg and Orchard Park to East Aurora and Wales. Traffic cameras showed snow covered roads and near whiteout conditions with several reports of cars sliding off the road. The band extended inland across southern Genesee and northern Wyoming counties to northern Livingston County. Snow totals of up to a foot were reported in 3 hours between 4 and 7 P.M. across southern Erie and Genesee Counties along with numerous reports of thunder snow in the vicinity of Orchard Park.  The band shifted back south under westerly winds during the evening and continued into the night. Snow totals of a bit over a foot accumulated across northern Chautauqua and Cattaraugus and southern Wyoming Counties. Specific reports included: 14 inches at West Valley, 13 inches at Perrysburg; 12 inches at Orchard Park and 11 inches at Varysburg and East Aurora.|Off Lake Ontario, the lake effect snow band formed Sunday evening with a long-axis parallel lake band focused directly east of the Lake across southern Jefferson and northern Oswego counties. The band maintained this position for about four hours before the band shifted south along the southern lake shore.  Snowfall rates of three to four inches per hour were observed by researchers studying lake effect snow in northern Oswego County which produced snow accumulations of 8 to 14 inches east of the lake before the band shifted south. During the early morning hours of Monday December 16th thunder snow was also recorded in the Lake Ontario snow band.  Through the remainder of the morning hours up to and past daybreak a very narrow and shallow lake band of less than five miles in wide hugged the southern Lake Ontario shoreline from Olcott in Niagara County to north of Rochester to the towns of Port Bay and Red Creek in far northeastern Wayne County. Specific snowfall reports included: 23 inches at Port Bay, 18 inches at Red Creek and 13 inches at Fair Haven and Lacona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front and associated pre frontal trough approached upstate New York during the afternoon hours on June 17th, 2013.  With temperatures into the 80s and high levels of moisture in place, there was plenty of instability available for thunderstorms to develop.  A line of thunderstorms developed across the Capital Region and strong winds aloft aided in allowing these storms to become severe with damaging winds and large hail.  By later in the afternoon, the thunderstorms tracked east and away from the region, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down in Granville due to thunderstorm winds.

An area of low pressure moved off the mid Atlantic coast Tuesday night, January 15th, and passed just south of Cape Cod Wednesday afternoon the 16th. Snow associated with this low overspread east central New York during the early morning hours Wednesday. Some warmer air worked into the area by early in the afternoon resulting in some sleet, freezing rain and freezing drizzle across the mid Hudson Valley. The bulk of the precipitation occurred in the morning in the form of snow and impacted the commute resulting in some accidents. Total snowfall reports ranged from 3 to 6 inches across the Schoharie Valley, the southern portion of the Capital District including Albany and Troy, eastern Catskills, mid Hudson Valley and Taconics.

The strongest storms produced one to two inch hailstones.  Specific hail reports were received from Stanley, Geneva, Newark, Walworth, Marion, and Brockport. In several of those locations, automobiles were damage by the hail stones.  Later in the afternoon thunderstorms rolled across the North Country with downed trees and power poles reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a warm and moist airmass in place over the region, thunderstorms developed along lake breezes during the afternoon and evening.  The persistent storms were prolific hail producers���not only in size but quantity. At times it was reported the hail completely covered the ground. The largest stones approached two inches, however most of the hail exceeded one-inch in diameter.  Hail was reported near Lake Bluff, Port Bay, Red Creek, Victory, Martville, West Monroe and Hastings, to name a few. While most of the severe reports were of large hail, a few storms did produce damaging winds with trees and wires reported down near Ira, Victory, Phoenix, Wellsville, Adams Center and Batavia.

A slow moving cold front and associated pre frontal trough approached upstate New York during the afternoon hours on June 17th, 2013.  With temperatures into the 80s and high levels of moisture in place, there was plenty of instability available for thunderstorms to develop.  A line of thunderstorms developed across the Capital Region and strong winds aloft aided in allowing these storms to become severe with damaging winds and large hail.  By later in the afternoon, the thunderstorms tracked east and away from the region, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down on Route 4 in Fort Edward due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved off the mid Atlantic coast Tuesday night, January 15th, and passed just south of Cape Cod Wednesday afternoon the 16th. Snow associated with this low overspread east central New York during the early morning hours Wednesday. Some warmer air worked into the area by early in the afternoon resulting in some sleet, freezing rain and freezing drizzle across the mid Hudson Valley. The bulk of the precipitation occurred in the morning in the form of snow and impacted the commute resulting in some accidents. Total snowfall reports ranged from 3 to 6 inches across the Schoharie Valley, the southern portion of the Capital District including Albany and Troy, eastern Catskills, mid Hudson Valley and Taconics.

The fourth lake effect event of the season was a combination of light synoptic snows followed lake effect snows.  Off Lake Erie, snow fell through the morning and afternoon hours of the 18th before migrating northward through the evening hours and dissipating. The lake effect band of snow off Lake Ontario intensified through the late morning and afternoon hours of the 18th while impacting the Tug Hill region. This band too also shifted northward overnight and weakened. Snowfall rates peaked at two inches per hour. Within the higher snowfall rates, thunder snow occurred. Gusty winds and the timing of early morning did bring accidents on area roads slowing the morning commute. Specific amounts reported included:  12 inches at West Falls and Highmarket; 11 inches as Boston and Lacona; 10 inches at Dunkirk and 8 inches as Constableville.
A cold front moving south resulted in northwest winds blowing across the warmer waters of Lake Ontario and produced significant lake effect snows in northern Oneida county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amount of 10 inches was reported eight miles north of Camden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough moving across Lake Ontario helped produce intense lake effect snow across northern Oneida county. The highest snowfall total of 9 inches occurred three miles west of Boonville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amount of 9 inches was reported three miles west of Boonville.

A surface front stalled across the region acted as a pathway for periods of heavy precipitation. South of the front, rain fell however to the north the precipitation fell as freezing rain. Ice coated trees, power lines and all exposed surfaces. Across the Niagara Frontier, ice accumulations of a half- to one-inch were reported. Across the north country, where the freezing rain persisted the longest, the ice accumulations ranged from one to two inches. The weight of the heavy ice brought down trees and power lines. In some cases, trees fell on homes, buildings and automobiles. Tens of thousands were left without power. New York State issued an Emergency Declaration for Jefferson and Lewis counties���the hardest hit of the region.

Low pressure moved from the Ohio Valley to the East Coast and brought a general accumulating snow to much of the region. Six to ten inches of snow blanketed the region with the higher amounts across the higher elevations of the Eastern Lake Ontario region and areas south of Lake Ontario where lake enhancement occurred.  The snow resulted in the usual traffic slowdowns and several accidents were blamed on the Specific snowfall amounts that were reported included: 10 inches at Lowville, Newark and Macedon; 9 inches at Theresa and Fulton; 8 inches at Constableville, Cato and Warsaw; and 7 inches at Rochester, Batavia, Minetto and Stafford.
A cold westerly flow produced significant lake effect snowfall in northern Oneida county in central New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow produced 9 to 14 inches. The highest snowfall total of 14 inches occurred in Boonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold northwest flow over Lake Ontario produced significant lake effect snow across central New York. Snowfall amounts ranged from 7 to 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow produced 7 to 10 inches. The highest snowfall total of 10.1 inches occurred 4 miles northeast of Baldwinsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold northwest flow over Lake Ontario produced significant lake effect snow across central New York. Snowfall amounts ranged from 7 to 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow produced 7 to 9 inches. The highest snowfall total of 8.6 inches occurred 2 miles east-southeast of Chittenango.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second lake effect event of the season occurred on the heels of the first.|Off Lake Erie, lake effect snow lifted north across the Buffalo metro area on Thursday December 12 during the rush hour commute.  The Buffalo Airport received a quick 6 for this event while areas to the north and west received up to a foot before the band moved south generally remaining in place through Friday.  The hardest hit areas included northwest Cattaraugus, northern Chautauqua, and southern and central Erie Counties.  Specific snowfall amounts included: 27 inches at Perrysburg, 23 inches at Glenwood, 22 inches at Warsaw, 14 inches at Dunkirk and 8 inches at Marilla.|Off Lake Ontario, a strong lake band developed and intensified just north of Watertown during the morning of 12th. Later in the afternoon, the band moved south back over the Tug Hill region and remained in place through daybreak Friday morning. Finally the band dropped south across Oswego county and dissipated during the morning as an arctic dry airmass settled into the region. The heaviest hit areas received four to six feet of snow. Specific reports included:  23 inches at Copenhagen, 22 inches at Redfield, 21 inches at Highmarket, and 17 inches at Lowville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region remained in a warm and humid air mass as an upper level ridge remained just south and east across the western Atlantic Ocean. At the same time, a weak upper level disturbance passed by to the north across southern Canada.  This upper level disturbance allowed showers and thunderstorms to develop across the region during the afternoon and early evening hours of Monday, June 24th.  With a moist and unstable atmosphere in place, some of these storms became severe, with large hail and damaging wind gusts.  Thunderstorms dissipated by later in the evening, as the disturbance moved east of the region and the atmosphere stabilized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North Creek Road near Kast Bridge was reportedly damaged due to flash flooding from heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid air mass was in place over eastern New York on Friday, July 19th. With temperatures well into the 90s, the air mass was very unstable.  ||A cold front was upstream of the region across the Great Lakes.  Ahead of this front, a complex of thunderstorms developed across Lake Ontario during the evening hours of July 19th.  As these thunderstorms moved eastward, they strengthened as they interacted with a lake-breeze boundary over central New York.  The thunderstorms raced eastward across the Mohawk Valley, southern Adirondacks, and Saratoga Region during the late evening hours.  Due to the quick movement of the storms and the very unstable air mass in place, the thunderstorms mainly produced damaging winds, although there was also a report of large hail and heavy rainfall, which led to a mudslide in Herkimer County.||The thunderstorms moved east of the region by midnight, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were reported down as a result of thunderstorm winds in Mohawk.

The fifth lake effect storm of the season was very similar to previous in that lake effect snows followed a general synoptic snowfall. Lake effect snow that developed off Lake Ontario developed across the Tug Hill region. The snow fell at the rate of two- to three-inches per hour at its peak. Specific snowfall amounts included:  20 inches at Lorraine; 16 inches at Highmarket; 12 inches at Hooker and 8 inches at Lacona.

The long-lived event started with lake effect developing during Tuesday morning and moving north and south across areas east of the lakes several times over the two-day event.|Off Lake Erie, a band of lake effect snow developed over the Chautauqua Ridge where as much as a half foot fell by afternoon. The band lifted into the Buffalo Metro area then settled back to the south later in the evening. During Wednesday, the band became quite intense with an extremely sharp edge on its northern boundary, cutting across Hamburg, East Aurora, and Elma. Snowfall rates reached three to five inches an hour in the most intense portion of the band.  The New York State Thruway was closed for several hours between Dunkirk and Hamburg. The band shifted south by evening and continued to snow throughout the night in the Boston Hills. Specific snowfall reports included:  35 inches at Colden, 26 inches at Wales and Boston, 15 inches at Dunkirk, and 8 inches at Stafford. |Off Lake Ontario, a similar story played out with an east-west oriented band developing and quickly Tuesday morning, dropping a half foot over upslope regions of the Tug Hill by Tuesday afternoon. The band intensified significantly Tuesday night, remaining in place with little movement through Wednesday night. Snowfall amounts of three to four feet fell in a 24- to 36-hour period.  Specific snowfall reports included:  44 inches at Redfield and Constableville, 20 inches at Bennetts Bridge, 18 inches at Mannsville and 16 inches at Lowville.

A very warm and humid air mass was in place over eastern New York on Friday, July 19th. With temperatures well into the 90s, the air mass was very unstable.  ||A cold front was upstream of the region across the Great Lakes.  Ahead of this front, a complex of thunderstorms developed across Lake Ontario during the evening hours of July 19th.  As these thunderstorms moved eastward, they strengthened as they interacted with a lake-breeze boundary over central New York.  The thunderstorms raced eastward across the Mohawk Valley, southern Adirondacks, and Saratoga Region during the late evening hours.  Due to the quick movement of the storms and the very unstable air mass in place, the thunderstorms mainly produced damaging winds, although there was also a report of large hail and heavy rainfall, which led to a mudslide in Herkimer County.||The thunderstorms moved east of the region by midnight, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and wires were reported down in Norway as a result of thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved off the mid Atlantic coast Tuesday night, January 15th, and passed just south of Cape Cod Wednesday afternoon the 16th. Snow associated with this low overspread east central New York during the early morning hours Wednesday. Some warmer air worked into the area by early in the afternoon resulting in some sleet, freezing rain and freezing drizzle across the mid Hudson Valley. The bulk of the precipitation occurred in the morning in the form of snow and impacted the commute resulting in some accidents. Total snowfall reports ranged from 3 to 6 inches across the Schoharie Valley, the southern portion of the Capital District including Albany and Troy, eastern Catskills, mid Hudson Valley and Taconics.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region was sandwiched between a strong stalled upper level low over the Ohio Valley and a ridge of high pressure situated over the western Atlantic Ocean.  The combined flow around both of these feature allowed a moist southerly flow directly out of the Caribbean Sea to move up the eastern seaboard.   Showers and thunderstorms developed along a very slow moving surface boundary and the persistent southerly flow caused a lot of these showers and thunderstorms to develop and pass over the same areas.  ||Due to heavy rainfall over the last month, the ground was rather saturated and it didn't take much additional rain to cause issues.  As a result, flash flooding occurred throughout the day on Monday, July 1st and again during the evening on Tuesday, July 2nd.  Roads were damaged and closed and some evacuations took place as homes were impacted by the flood waters.  The hardest hit areas were across part of the Mohawk Valley, where significant flash flooding had occurred just a few days prior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gage at Kast Bridge recorded flash flooding as a result of heavy rainfall. The river gage crested at 7.85 feet at 9:30 PM EST, which is considered moderate flooding. The river gage at Kast Bridge is located along on the right bank of the West Canada Creek 600 feet downstream from a bridge on old State Highway 28 at Kast Bridge, 1.2 miles downstream from North Creek, 2.2 miles north of Herkimer, and 4.0 miles upstream from the river's mouth at the Mohawk River.

The first major lake effect event of the 2013-2014 seasoned occurred over the areas east of Lakes Erie and Ontario beginning Tuesday, December 10.  The long-lived event started with lake effect developing during Tuesday morning and moving north and south across areas east of the lakes several times over the two-day event.|Off Lake Erie, a band of lake effect snow developed over the Chautauqua Ridge where as much as a half foot fell by afternoon. The band lifted into the Buffalo Metro area then settled back to the south later in the evening. Snowfall rates reached three to five inches an hour in the most intense portion of the band.  The New York State Thruway was closed for several hours between Dunkirk and Hamburg. The band shifted south by evening and continued to snow throughout the night in the Boston Hills. Specific snowfall reports included:  35 inches at Colden, 26 inches at Wales and Boston, 15 inches at Dunkirk, and 8 inches at Stafford. |Off Lake Ontario, a similar story played out with an east-west oriented band developing and quickly Tuesday morning, dropping a half foot over upslope regions of the Tug Hill by Tuesday afternoon. The band intensified significantly Tuesday night, remaining in place with little movement through Wednesday night. Snowfall amounts of three to four feet fell in a 24- to 36-hour period.  Specific snowfall reports included:  44 inches at Redfield and Constableville, 20 inches at Bennetts Bridge, 18 inches at Mannsville and 16 inches at Lowville.

A warm front associated with a low pressure system that moved across the Ohio Valley and Pennsylvania resulted in a swath of one to two inches of rain from the Rochester metro area east to near Fulton. This created minor flooding issues on area roadways along with flooding of Ellison Park in Rochester when Irondequoit Creek rose above bankful. The creek crested at 9.23 feet around 4PM on the 14th and receded to its banks on the morning of the 15th.

A warm and humid air mass was in place across the region on the evening on Saturday, June 1st into the day on Sunday, June 2nd.  Daytime temperatures reached well into the 80s across much of the area. A cold front and associated pre-frontal surface trough was moving towards the area over the weekend. With plenty of moisture and instability in place, showers and thunderstorms developed across the region. These thunderstorms were aided by very strong winds aloft and a few storms became severe across eastern New York, producing wind damage and large hail. The thunderstorms weakened by evening as the cold front moved across the area, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on South Shore Road about 5 miles east of Old Forge as a result of thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon in a warm, humid air mass. A thunderstorm over Oswego County produced hail up to quarter-size in diameter. Reports received from the public said that hail fell for fifteen to twenty minutes.

Thunderstorms developed during the morning along a lake breeze. The nearly stationary thunderstorm dropped one to two inches of rain over the Towns of Brocton, Pomfret and Portland. Over 40 homes were evacuated because of flood waters with several roads and bridges damaged. The Town of Brocton���s reservoir was also damaged.

A complex area of low pressure lifted across New York and then stalled near Quebec. The low brought periods of rain with embedded heavier showers and thunderstorms.  A nearly stationary boundary lying east-west across Niagara County was the focus for persistent heavy storms during the afternoon. Rain began shortly after noon and continued through eight in the evening. The heaviest rain fell during the two-period between one and three. Rainfall amounts across the southern half of the county totaled one to two inches however the City of Lockport and the southeast corner of the county received around five inches of rain. The city���s sewer and water treatment plant were overwhelmed by the water. Normally treating about seven million gallons a day, the plant treated more than 90 million gallons. In some locations, the force of the water going through the system blew covers off the manholes.  Many city streets were damaged with pavement being completely washed away in some locations.  Over 600 homes and dozens of small businesses were affected in Lockport. The heavy rain extended into northern Erie County where numerous roads were also flooded. Major arteries including Maple and Niagara Falls Boulevard were inundated.

The persistent storms were prolific hail producers���not only in size but quantity. At times it was reported the hail completely covered the ground. The largest stones approached two inches, however most of the hail exceeded one-inch in diameter.  Hail was reported near Lake Bluff, Port Bay, Red Creek, Victory, Martville, West Monroe and Hastings, to name a few. While most of the severe reports were of large hail, a few storms did produce damaging winds with trees and wires reported down near Ira, Victory, Phoenix, Wellsville, Adams Center and Batavia.

A warm and humid air mass was in place across the region on the evening on Saturday, June 1st into the day on Sunday, June 2nd.  Daytime temperatures reached well into the 80s across much of the area. A cold front and associated pre-frontal surface trough was moving towards the area over the weekend. With plenty of moisture and instability in place, showers and thunderstorms developed across the region. These thunderstorms were aided by very strong winds aloft and a few storms became severe across eastern New York, producing wind damage and large hail. The thunderstorms weakened by evening as the cold front moved across the area, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported via Twitter during a thunderstorm in Glens Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region remained in a warm and humid air mass as an upper level ridge remained just south and east across the western Atlantic Ocean. At the same time, a weak upper level disturbance passed by to the north across southern Canada.  This upper level disturbance allowed showers and thunderstorms to develop across the region during the afternoon and early evening hours of Monday, June 24th.  With a moist and unstable atmosphere in place, some of these storms became severe, with large hail and damaging wind gusts.  Thunderstorms dissipated by later in the evening, as the disturbance moved east of the region and the atmosphere stabilized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported during a thunderstorm in Ravena.

The first major lake effect event of the 2013-2014 seasoned occurred over the areas east of Lakes Erie and Ontario beginning Tuesday, December 10.  The long-lived event started with lake effect developing during Tuesday morning and moving north and south across areas east of the lakes several times over the two-day event.|Off Lake Erie, a band of lake effect snow developed over the Chautauqua Ridge where as much as a half foot fell by afternoon. The band lifted into the Buffalo Metro area then settled back to the south later in the evening. During Wednesday, the band became quite intense with an extremely sharp edge on its northern boundary, cutting across Hamburg, East Aurora, and Elma. Snowfall rates reached three to five inches an hour in the most intense portion of the band.  The New York State Thruway was closed for several hours between Dunkirk and Hamburg. The band shifted south by evening and continued to snow throughout the night in the Boston Hills. Specific snowfall reports included:  35 inches at Colden, 26 inches at Wales and Boston, 15 inches at Dunkirk, and 8 inches at Stafford. |Off Lake Ontario, a similar story played out with an east-west oriented band developing and quickly Tuesday morning, dropping a half foot over upslope regions of the Tug Hill by Tuesday afternoon. The band intensified significantly Tuesday night, remaining in place with little movement through Wednesday night. Snowfall amounts of three to four feet fell in a 24- to 36-hour period.  Specific snowfall reports included:  44 inches at Redfield and Constableville, 20 inches at Bennetts Bridge, 18 inches at Mannsville and 16 inches at Lowville.

Two distinct lines of thunderstorms developed ahead of a weak boundary during the late afternoon and became more organized toward evening. One line developed across the Genesee Valley and the other across the eastern Lake Ontario Region. Thunderstorm winds downed trees in Ridgeway, Murray, Ogden Center, Ellery Center, Belleville, Croghan, Lowville and near the Chautauqua Institution.  In some areas, scattered power outages were reported as the falling limbs and trees brought down power lines. In Rochester, lighting struck a house igniting a fire and damaging the chimney.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported that house was damaged by a lightning strike.

A low pressure system developed over the southern Plains on December 13th and intensified as it headed toward the northeast U.S. A coastal low developed along the Atlantic seaboard on December 15th. Warm advection snow developed on the morning of Saturday. December 14th along a stationary boundary stretched across the Mason-Dixon line and dropped an initial 1 to 3 inches of snow. As the low pressure system intensified, it produced moderate to heavy snowfall across the western Catskills and the upper Susquehanna Region of New York. The highest snowfall was reported in Masonville in Delaware County and Oneonta in Otsego County with 12 inches of snow reported at each location.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 8 to 11 inches. The highest amount of 11 inches fell in Smithville Flats.

A very warm and humid air mass was in place over eastern New York on Friday, July 19th. With temperatures well into the 90s, the air mass was very unstable.  ||A cold front was upstream of the region across the Great Lakes.  Ahead of this front, a complex of thunderstorms developed across Lake Ontario during the evening hours of July 19th.  As these thunderstorms moved eastward, they strengthened as they interacted with a lake-breeze boundary over central New York.  The thunderstorms raced eastward across the Mohawk Valley, southern Adirondacks, and Saratoga Region during the late evening hours.  Due to the quick movement of the storms and the very unstable air mass in place, the thunderstorms mainly produced damaging winds, although there was also a report of large hail and heavy rainfall, which led to a mudslide in Herkimer County.||The thunderstorms moved east of the region by midnight, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down in Herkimer as a result of thunderstorm winds.

Due to the quick movement of the storms and the very unstable air mass in place, the thunderstorms mainly produced damaging winds, although there was also a report of large hail and heavy rainfall, which led to a mudslide in Herkimer County.||The thunderstorms moved east of the region by midnight, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down in Dolgeville as a result of thunderstorm winds.

A low pressure system developed over the southern Plains on December 13th and intensified as it headed toward the northeast U.S. A coastal low developed along the Atlantic seaboard on December 15th. Warm advection snow developed on the morning of Saturday. December 14th along a stationary boundary stretched across the Mason-Dixon line and dropped an initial 1 to 3 inches of snow. As the low pressure system intensified, it produced moderate to heavy snowfall across the western Catskills and the upper Susquehanna Region of New York. The highest snowfall was reported in Masonville in Delaware County and Oneonta in Otsego County with 12 inches of snow reported at each location.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 8 to 12 inches. The highest amount of 12 inches was reported in Oneonta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from the Ohio Valley to the East Coast and brought a general accumulating snow to much of the region. Six to ten inches of snow blanketed the region with the higher amounts across the higher elevations of the Eastern Lake Ontario region and areas south of Lake Ontario where lake enhancement occurred.  The snow resulted in the usual traffic slowdowns and several accidents were blamed on the Specific snowfall amounts that were reported included: 10 inches at Lowville, Newark and Macedon; 9 inches at Theresa and Fulton; 8 inches at Constableville, Cato and Warsaw; and 7 inches at Rochester, Batavia, Minetto and Stafford.

The first major lake effect event of the 2013-2014 seasoned occurred over the areas east of Lakes Erie and Ontario beginning Tuesday, December 10.  The long-lived event started with lake effect developing during Tuesday morning and moving north and south across areas east of the lakes several times over the two-day event.|Off Lake Erie, a band of lake effect snow developed over the Chautauqua Ridge where as much as a half foot fell by afternoon. The band lifted into the Buffalo Metro area then settled back to the south later in the evening. During Wednesday, the band became quite intense with an extremely sharp edge on its northern boundary, cutting across Hamburg, East Aurora, and Elma. Snowfall rates reached three to five inches an hour in the most intense portion of the band.  The New York State Thruway was closed for several hours between Dunkirk and Hamburg. The band shifted south by evening and continued to snow throughout the night in the Boston Hills. Specific snowfall reports included:  35 inches at Colden, 26 inches at Wales and Boston, 15 inches at Dunkirk, and 8 inches at Stafford. |Off Lake Ontario, a similar story played out with an east-west oriented band developing and quickly Tuesday morning, dropping a half foot over upslope regions of the Tug Hill by Tuesday afternoon. The band intensified significantly Tuesday night, remaining in place with little movement through Wednesday night. Snowfall amounts of three to four feet fell in a 24- to 36-hour period.  Specific snowfall reports included:  44 inches at Redfield and Constableville, 20 inches at Bennetts Bridge, 18 inches at Mannsville and 16 inches at Lowville.

Two distinct lines of thunderstorms developed ahead of a weak boundary during the late afternoon and became more organized toward evening. One line developed across the Genesee Valley and the other across the eastern Lake Ontario Region. Thunderstorm winds downed trees in Ridgeway, Murray, Ogden Center, Ellery Center, Belleville, Croghan, Lowville and near the Chautauqua Institution.  In some areas, scattered power outages were reported as the falling limbs and trees brought down power lines. In Rochester, lighting struck a house igniting a fire and damaging the chimney.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down in Ridgeway.

With a warm and moist airmass in place over the region, thunderstorms developed along lake breezes during the afternoon and evening.  The persistent storms were prolific hail producers���not only in size but quantity. At times it was reported the hail completely covered the ground. The largest stones approached two inches, however most of the hail exceeded one-inch in diameter.  Hail was reported near Lake Bluff, Port Bay, Red Creek, Victory, Martville, West Monroe and Hastings, to name a few. While most of the severe reports were of large hail, a few storms did produce damaging winds with trees and wires reported down near Ira, Victory, Phoenix, Wellsville, Adams Center and Batavia.

The region remained in a warm and humid air mass as an upper level ridge remained just south and east across the western Atlantic Ocean. At the same time, a weak upper level disturbance passed by to the north across southern Canada.  This upper level disturbance allowed showers and thunderstorms to develop across the region during the afternoon and early evening hours of Monday, June 24th.  With a moist and unstable atmosphere in place, some of these storms became severe, with large hail and damaging wind gusts.  Thunderstorms dissipated by later in the evening, as the disturbance moved east of the region and the atmosphere stabilized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down due to thunderstorm winds in West Galway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along various boundaries within a warm and moist air mass over the region. Storms first developed across the Finger Lakes midday along the Lake Ontario lake breeze and then in the mid afternoon along the Lake Erie lake breeze boundary as it pushed inland across the Southern Tier. The strongest storms produced one to two inch hailstones.  Specific hail reports were received from Stanley, Geneva, Newark, Walworth, Marion, and Brockport. In several of those locations, automobiles were damage by the hail stones.  Later in the afternoon thunderstorms rolled across the North Country with downed trees and power poles reported.

A persistent upper level trough over eastern Canada kept a cold westerly flow over the Great Lakes. The event started Sunday afternoon (December 15th) to the east of Lake Erie during the afternoon hours. The snow fell at up to three inches per hour with near zero visibility in a 15-mile wide band from Hamburg and Orchard Park to East Aurora and Wales. Traffic cameras showed snow covered roads and near whiteout conditions with several reports of cars sliding off the road. The band extended inland across southern Genesee and northern Wyoming counties to northern Livingston County. Snow totals of up to a foot were reported in 3 hours between 4 and 7 P.M. across southern Erie and Genesee Counties along with numerous reports of thunder snow in the vicinity of Orchard Park.  The band shifted back south under westerly winds during the evening and continued into the night. Snow totals of a bit over a foot accumulated across northern Chautauqua and Cattaraugus and southern Wyoming Counties. Specific reports included: 14 inches at West Valley, 13 inches at Perrysburg; 12 inches at Orchard Park and 11 inches at Varysburg and East Aurora.|Off Lake Ontario, the lake effect snow band formed Sunday evening with a long-axis parallel lake band focused directly east of the Lake across southern Jefferson and northern Oswego counties. The band maintained this position for about four hours before the band shifted south along the southern lake shore.  Snowfall rates of three to four inches per hour were observed by researchers studying lake effect snow in northern Oswego County which produced snow accumulations of 8 to 14 inches east of the lake before the band shifted south. During the early morning hours of Monday December 16th thunder snow was also recorded in the Lake Ontario snow band.  Through the remainder of the morning hours up to and past daybreak a very narrow and shallow lake band of less than five miles in wide hugged the southern Lake Ontario shoreline from Olcott in Niagara County to north of Rochester to the towns of Port Bay and Red Creek in far northeastern Wayne County. Specific snowfall reports included: 23 inches at Port Bay, 18 inches at Red Creek and 13 inches at Fair Haven and Lacona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The fourth lake effect event of the season was a combination of light synoptic snows followed lake effect snows.  Off Lake Erie, snow fell through the morning and afternoon hours of the 18th before migrating northward through the evening hours and dissipating. The lake effect band of snow off Lake Ontario intensified through the late morning and afternoon hours of the 18th while impacting the Tug Hill region. This band too also shifted northward overnight and weakened. Snowfall rates peaked at two inches per hour. Within the higher snowfall rates, thunder snow occurred. Gusty winds and the timing of early morning did bring accidents on area roads slowing the morning commute. Specific amounts reported included:  12 inches at West Falls and Highmarket; 11 inches as Boston and Lacona; 10 inches at Dunkirk and 8 inches as Constableville.

After a week of late summer warmth and humidity, a cold front crossed the region. The front sparked showers and thunderstorms with its passage. Several of the thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds with the large hail mainly across the southern tier and the damaging wind gusts across the north country.  Hail, up to 1.75 inch in diameter, was reported in Jamestown, Bemus Point, Lakewood, Jordan, Red Creek, Marion and Oswego. The thunderstorms winds downed trees and power lines. At the Chautauqua Lake Yacht Club in Lakewood, several boats were flipped in addition to the down limbs and wires. In several locations, downed trees made roads impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region was sandwiched between a strong stalled upper level low over the Ohio Valley and a ridge of high pressure situated over the western Atlantic Ocean.  The combined flow around both of these feature allowed a moist southerly flow directly out of the Caribbean Sea to move up the eastern seaboard.   Showers and thunderstorms developed along a very slow moving surface boundary and the persistent southerly flow caused a lot of these showers and thunderstorms to develop and pass over the same areas.  ||Due to heavy rainfall over the last month, the ground was rather saturated and it didn't take much additional rain to cause issues.  As a result, flash flooding occurred throughout the day on Monday, July 1st and again during the evening on Tuesday, July 2nd.  Roads were damaged and closed and some evacuations took place as homes were impacted by the flood waters.  The hardest hit areas were across part of the Mohawk Valley, where significant flash flooding had occurred just a few days prior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Vichy Drive in Saratoga Springs was closed due to flash flooding from heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid air mass was in place over the region on Friday, July 5th.  This allowed the atmosphere to become quite unstable.  A weak upper level disturbance passing close to the region, along with daytime heating, allowed showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and evening hours.  As these showers and thunderstorms repeatedly tracked over Washington County, small streams and creeks were overwhelmed by the rainfall, causing flash flooding to occur. Several roads were closed as a result of the flooding. By the late evening, thunderstorms dissipated and water quickly receded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several streets, including portions of John Street and State Route 196, in Hudson Falls were closed due to flash flooding from heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As low pressure moved east of the region, cold arctic air filtered into western and north central New York. Away from the immediate lake shores, temperatures fell below freezing.  In some areas, unprotected vegetation was damaged by the freezing temperatures though the freeze was not widespread enough to end the current growing season. Specific minimum readings included: 29 degrees at Honeoye Falls, Attica  and Bristol; 30 degrees at Colden; 31 degrees at Batavia; and 32 degrees at Dansville, Lockport, Albion and Lakewood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid air mass was in place over eastern New York on Friday, July 19th. With temperatures well into the 90s, the air mass was very unstable.  ||A cold front was upstream of the region across the Great Lakes.  Ahead of this front, a complex of thunderstorms developed across Lake Ontario during the evening hours of July 19th.  As these thunderstorms moved eastward, they strengthened as they interacted with a lake-breeze boundary over central New York.  The thunderstorms raced eastward across the Mohawk Valley, southern Adirondacks, and Saratoga Region during the late evening hours.  Due to the quick movement of the storms and the very unstable air mass in place, the thunderstorms mainly produced damaging winds, although there was also a report of large hail and heavy rainfall, which led to a mudslide in Herkimer County.||The thunderstorms moved east of the region by midnight, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down in Wilton via Facebook as a result of thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and very humid air mass was in place on the evening of Monday, July 8th. A few isolated heavy showers and thunderstorm developed and moved across eastern New York. One of these thunderstorms strengthened as it passed over the urbanized areas of Albany County and produced very heavy rainfall in a short period of time. This caused flash flooding in Colonie and Albany, including portions of the University at Albany campus. Flood waters quickly receded after thunderstorm activity ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many roads were closed in the town of Colonie as a result of flash flooding from heavy rainfall.

A very warm and humid air mass was in place on Thursday, July 18th.  With temperatures exceeding 90 degrees, the atmosphere was very unstable.  A weak upper level disturbance passed to the north of the area across southern Canada. Isolated thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours.  One of these thunderstorms grew tall enough to become severe, producing wind damage to trees in Columbia County.  Thunderstorm activity diminished in the evening with the loss of daytime heating as the weak upper level disturbance moved away from the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down in Green River as a result of thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon and evening of May 22nd, a stationary boundary remained draped across northern New York. There was enough low-level instability, as well as disturbances along this boundary to produce isolated thunderstorms during the evening hours in the Champlain Valley that produced large hail and scattered thunderstorms toward the overnight in St. Lawrence county that produced scattered wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of quarter-size hail along the northern portions of Lake Placid.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 20th, 2013, a very warm and humid air mass was in place over eastern New York, allowing for a rather unstable atmosphere.  A cold front, with somewhat cooler and less humid air in its wake, was approaching the region from the Great Lakes.  Ahead of this boundary, isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours.  One of these thunderstorms became severe across Dutchess County, producing wind damage.  ||With the passage of the cold front, thunderstorm activity diminished by the late afternoon and the threat for severe weather ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power wire was reported down as a result of thunderstorm winds in the town of North East.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon and evening of May 22nd, a stationary boundary remained draped across northern New York. There was enough low-level instability, as well as disturbances along this boundary to produce isolated thunderstorms during the evening hours in the Champlain Valley that produced large hail and scattered thunderstorms toward the overnight in St. Lawrence county that produced scattered wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees and power lines down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a week of late summer warmth and humidity, a cold front crossed the region. The front sparked showers and thunderstorms with its passage. Several of the thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds with the large hail mainly across the southern tier and the damaging wind gusts across the north country.  Hail, up to 1.75 inch in diameter, was reported in Jamestown, Bemus Point, Lakewood, Jordan, Red Creek, Marion and Oswego. The thunderstorms winds downed trees and power lines. At the Chautauqua Lake Yacht Club in Lakewood, several boats were flipped in addition to the down limbs and wires. In several locations, downed trees made roads impassable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees and wires down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure brought clearing skies and light winds allowing overnight temperatures to fall well into the twenties. A widespread killing freeze occurred across the entire region ending the 2013 growing season in western and north-central New York. Specific overnight lows recorded included: 31 at Whitesville; 28 at Colden; 27 at Forestville and Hinsdale; 26 degrees at Albion and Tylersville; 25 degrees at Lancaster and the Iroquois Refuge; 24 at Livonia; 23 at Lyons, Port Byron and Canandaigua; 22 degrees at Middleport and Central Square;  and 20 degrees at Honeoye Falls.

A series of cold front moved towards the region on Thursday, September 12th.  Despite some periods of cloudiness, a warm and humid air mass ahead of the approaching boundaries allowed for moderate amounts of instability to be in place.  Along and ahead of the boundaries, several lines of showers and thunderstorms developed and moved across the region during the afternoon and early evening hours.  In addition to a large amount of cloud to ground lightning, a few of the thunderstorms became severe, with damaging wind gusts.  Several trees were downed across the region.  Up to 4,600 people were without power at one point in the Capital Region due to the thunderstorms.||Some areas that received repeated showers and thunderstorms experienced flash flooding as well, with roads closed as a result.  The flooding even caused an evacuation of a senior hosing complex in Nassau.  Two to four inches of rain in a short period of time was reported in the areas that experienced flash flooding.||As the last in the series of cold fronts crossed during the evening hours, the threat for showers and thunderstorms ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall led to flash flooding at the intersection of Canvass and Oneida Streets in Cohoes. Two to three feet of standing water was ponded in the intersection of the road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous tree limbs were blown down. Trained spotter specified the tree limbs were not rotted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front across central New York resulted in a severe thunderstorm developing across the area.

The region was sandwiched between a strong stalled upper level low over the Ohio Valley and a ridge of high pressure situated over the western Atlantic Ocean.  The combined flow around both of these feature allowed a moist southerly flow directly out of the Caribbean Sea to move up the eastern seaboard.   Showers and thunderstorms developed along a very slow moving surface boundary and the persistent southerly flow caused a lot of these showers and thunderstorms to develop and pass over the same areas.  ||Due to heavy rainfall over the last month, the ground was rather saturated and it didn't take much additional rain to cause issues.  As a result, flash flooding occurred throughout the day on Monday, July 1st and again during the evening on Tuesday, July 2nd.  Roads were damaged and closed and some evacuations took place as homes were impacted by the flood waters.  The hardest hit areas were across part of the Mohawk Valley, where significant flash flooding had occurred just a few days prior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads were closed throughout Ilion due to flash flooding from heavy rainfall.  Areas along the Steele Creek were impacted the most.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure brought clear skies and light winds to the region. Across the north country, temperatures fell below freezing. A low of 30 degrees was reported in Watertown and 29 degrees in Lowville.

A slow moving cold front crossed the region and brought a widespread soaking rainfall to much of the region. Rainfall amounts of two- to three-and-a-half-inches fell across the Niagara Frontier. The heavy rains resulted in urban flooding in parts of northern Erie County.
Showers and thunderstorms accompanied the passage of a short wave trough across the area. The thunderstorms produced hail up to one-inch in diameter. Hail was reported in Kent, Greece and Clarence.

Up to 4,600 people were without power at one point in the Capital Region due to the thunderstorms.||Some areas that received repeated showers and thunderstorms experienced flash flooding as well, with roads closed as a result.  The flooding even caused an evacuation of a senior hosing complex in Nassau.  Two to four inches of rain in a short period of time was reported in the areas that experienced flash flooding.||As the last in the series of cold fronts crossed during the evening hours, the threat for showers and thunderstorms ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kinderhook Creek overflowed its banks and flooded Chatham Street in Nassau.  The water also rose and affected a senior housing complex, which prompted an evacuation of the building.

A thunderstorm that developed along convergent boundaries in Cattaraugus County produced hail up to one-and-one-quarter-inch in diameter near Freedom.

A line of showers and thunderstorms developed over Southern Ontario and rapidly moved southeast across the Niagara Frontier. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds that downed trees and power lines in Bergholtz.  In Cheektowaga, lightning struck a power transformer in the vicinity of French Road. The media reported live wires down along with power out in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported wires down by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a week of late summer warmth and humidity, a cold front crossed the region. The front sparked showers and thunderstorms with its passage. Several of the thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds with the large hail mainly across the southern tier and the damaging wind gusts across the north country.  Hail, up to 1.75 inch in diameter, was reported in Jamestown, Bemus Point, Lakewood, Jordan, Red Creek, Marion and Oswego. The thunderstorms winds downed trees and power lines. At the Chautauqua Lake Yacht Club in Lakewood, several boats were flipped in addition to the down limbs and wires. In several locations, downed trees made roads impassable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported trees and wires down.

As low pressure moved east of the region, cold arctic air filtered into western and north central New York. Away from the immediate lake shores, temperatures fell below freezing.  In some areas, unprotected vegetation was damaged by the freezing temperatures though the freeze was not widespread enough to end the current growing season. Specific minimum readings included: 29 degrees at Honeoye Falls, Attica  and Bristol; 30 degrees at Colden; 31 degrees at Batavia; and 32 degrees at Dansville, Lockport, Albion and Lakewood.

A series of cold front moved towards the region on Thursday, September 12th.  Despite some periods of cloudiness, a warm and humid air mass ahead of the approaching boundaries allowed for moderate amounts of instability to be in place.  Along and ahead of the boundaries, several lines of showers and thunderstorms developed and moved across the region during the afternoon and early evening hours.  In addition to a large amount of cloud to ground lightning, a few of the thunderstorms became severe, with damaging wind gusts.  Several trees were downed across the region.  Up to 4,600 people were without power at one point in the Capital Region due to the thunderstorms.||Some areas that received repeated showers and thunderstorms experienced flash flooding as well, with roads closed as a result.  The flooding even caused an evacuation of a senior hosing complex in Nassau.  Two to four inches of rain in a short period of time was reported in the areas that experienced flash flooding.||As the last in the series of cold fronts crossed during the evening hours, the threat for showers and thunderstorms ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding and a mudslide occurred on Route 9J, just south of the intersection with Route 26A, in Stuyvesant, as a result of heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass settled in over the region on January 22nd and remained in place for a few days as cold fronts moved across the region ushering in reinforcing surges of cold air. In addition to the cold air, winds remained rather brisk for much of the time resulting in dangerously cold wind chill values. Minimum temperatures dropped below zero across most east central New York each night and during the day temperatures only warmed into the single digits to teens.

A strong arctic cold front passed through the region on Sunday, January 20th, 2013.  As this cold front passed by, much colder air aloft moved into the area.  As the cooling aloft combined with the heating of the sun in the wake of the front, the atmosphere became mixed and allowed strong, gusty winds to reach the surface.  Winds gusted to 60 mph across portions of the Mohawk Valley, southern Adirondacks, and Saratoga Region during the late morning and early afternoon hours.  This caused downed trees and power lines, as well as some power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power outages from downed wires were reported throughout Hamilton County due to high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over the northern Finger Lakes along a lake breeze in a warm humid air mass. The thunderstorms produce damaging winds which downed trees and power lines. These were reported in Shelby Center, Fairport, Pittsford, Macedon, Palmyra, Newark and Lyons. The thunderstorms also produced hail up to one inch in diameter in Waterport. Between one-and-one-half and two-and-one ���half inches of rain was measured across parts of Monroe and Wayne Counties. This amount of rain in a very short time resulted in poor drainage flooding in the City of Rochester. Several city streets were inundated, included Amsterdam Road and Monroe Avenue.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement received multiple reports of trees and wires downed by thunderstorm winds.

Lake snows persisted as a deep trough of low pressure became entrenched across the northeast United States. |Off Lake Erie, a band of lake effect snow developed along the Lake Erie shoreline Monday (21st) producing very heavy snow and difficult travel along the New York State Thruway corridor from Hamburg to the Pennsylvania State Line. Snowfall rates during this time reached about two inches per hour. From Tuesday through Thursday, multiple bands of somewhat lighter lake effect snow continued across much of the Southern Tier and occasionally drifted into southern Erie and Wyoming counties. The greatest totals off Lake Erie were found in southwest Chautauqua County and along the Chautauqua ridge where snow totals were near two feet. Specific snowfall reports included:  25 inches at Perrysburg and Ripley, 21 inches at Forestville, 18 inches at Glenwood, 16 inches at West Valley, 13 inches at Warsaw and 12 inches at Collins.|Off Lake Ontario, an intense band of lake effect snow developed east of the lake late Monday Night across the Tug Hill region and drifted slowly south across Oswego County on Tuesday producing more heavy snow along the way. Snowfall rates reached three to five inches per hour during this time. The band then settled along the south shore of the lake Tuesday Night with heavy snow along the entire south shore. Periodic bands of somewhat weaker lake effect snow then continued along the south shore of the lake through midweek. The greatest totals off Lake Ontario were found over Oswego County ��� nearing two feet. About a foot fell along much of the south shore within a few miles of the lake from Eastern Niagara County to Wayne County.  Specific snowfall reports included:  22 inches at Palermo and Phoenix, 20 inches at Fulton, 18 inches at Fair Haven, 15 inches at Osceola, 13 inches at Lyndonville, 12 inches at Sodus, 11 inches at Hamlin Beach, 10 inches at Newfane.

A cold airmass over the warmer waters of Lakes Erie and Ontario produced lake effect snows off both lakes. |Off Lake Erie, a disorganized band of lake effect snow began across far southern Erie and Wyoming counties then shifted south, becoming better organized. Snowfall amounts were diminished by some ice cover, however they ranged from eight to thirteen inches across the higher elevations of Chautauqua and Cattaraugus counties. |Off Lake Ontario, the lake effect plume that formed also had an upstream connection to Lake Huron which enhanced snowfall amounts. Snowfall rates in excess of two inches an hour dropped several feet of snow across Northern Cayuga and Oswego Counties. Specific reports included 33 inches at Fulton, 25 inches at Fair Haven and Sterling, and 17 inches at Phoenix.

An area of low pressure passing over the lower Great Lakes brought a general half- to one-foot snowfall across the northern sections of the region. Along the south shore of Lake Ontario amounts were enhanced by additional moisture from the lake and totals ranged from a foot to a foot and a half. Many schools were closed on Friday the 8th.  The snowfall made for a slow, messy commute for the Buffalo, Niagara Falls and Rochester metro areas.  Specific snowfall totals included: 18 inches at Gasport; 17 inches at Lockport; 16 inches at Irondequoit, Greece, and Sandborn; 15 inches at Grand Island, Henderson, and Spencerport; 14 inches at Lewiston, Holley, Newark and Youngstown; 13 inches at West Carthage, Niagara Falls, Marion and Walworth; 12 inches at Tonawanda, Lowville, Constableville, and Rochester Airport; 11 inches at Fulton, Oakfield and Lyndonville; 10 inches at Swormville, Kenmore, and Highmarket; 9 inches at Alden; 8 inches at Montezuma and Buffalo airport; and 7 inches at Redfield.

Showers and thunderstorms developed in a warm, moist, unstable air mass as a cold front approached from the west.  The thunderstorms produced damaging winds. Hail up to one-inch in diameter fell near Sherman. The thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines. In Cherry Creek and Leon, both in Cattaraugus County, several roads were blocked by fallen trees. In Springwater, in Livingston County, a house was damaged when thunderstorm winds knocked a tree onto it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down by thunderstorm winds.

A strong and deepening area of low pressure moved across Ontario and Quebec provinces on November 1st. The associated cold front moved across northern New York during the morning to early afternoon hours and delivered strong southwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts in excess of 60 mph. These winds caused numerous reports of tree branches and trees down as well as on utility lines that caused power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of tree branches and trees down across roads or utility lines across northern Franklin county, including towns of Malone, Constable and Hogansburg.

This caused downed trees and power lines, as well as some power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust was estimated to 60 mph at 10:39 AM EST by a trained spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved through central New York Saturday afternoon, November 23rd. This front brought a very cold northwest flow of air to central New York. This led to significant lake effect snows across portions of central New York through the afternoon hours of November 24th. Snow amounts ranged from 7 to 12 inches with the highest total of 11.5 inches occurring in Sempronius, NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amount of 11.5 inches was reported in Sempronius, NY.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening storm system moved across the Upper Great lakes. The system brought strong, damaging winds to the entire region late Saturday night into Sunday (20th-21st). Trees, power poles and wires were brought down by the winds. Numerous roads were blocked by fallen trees, wires and debris. Some structural damage was also reported. Some of the more significant reported included: an awning ripped off in Lancaster, roof trusses were downed on an building under construction in Batavia, part of a metal roof blown off in Portageville, a trailer was damaged by a fallen tree in West Bloomfield, ladder assembly blown off a silo near Constableville, and a boat was destroyed by a fallen tree in Livonia. Utilities reported tens of thousands without power for a time. Specific measured gusts included:  77 mph at Oswego lake shore; 69 mph at Dunkirk; 66 mph at Olcott; 62 mph at Barcelona and Watertown; 61 mph at Buffalo Airport and Niagara Falls Airport; 59 mph at Jamestown, Rochester Airport, and Youngstown; 55 mph at Wellsville; 54 mph at Fort Drum; 52 mph at Fulton; and 51 mph at Livonia.
A strong surface cold front approached from the west and developed severe convection across upstate New York during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was accompanied by 30 to 40 MPH wind gusts.

Showers and thunderstorms developed in a warm, moist, unstable air mass as a cold front approached from the west.  The thunderstorms produced damaging winds. Hail up to one-inch in diameter fell near Sherman. The thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines. In Cherry Creek and Leon, both in Cattaraugus County, several roads were blocked by fallen trees. In Springwater, in Livingston County, a house was damaged when thunderstorm winds knocked a tree onto it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported power lines down by thunderstorm winds.

After an area of low pressure crossed the Upper Great Lakes, lake effect snows developed off Lake Ontario under a cold, northwest flow. A band of snow developed over the southeast segment of the lake producing heavy snow across parts of Wayne, Northern Cayuga and Oswego counties Sunday evening through the early overnight hours. The band drifted north over Lake Ontario and weakened Monday Morning (21st). Snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour occurred. Specific snowfall amounts included:  18 inches as Fair Haven and Fulton, 15 inches as Palermo and Phoenix and 8 inches at Wolcott.

An area of low pressure passing over the lower Great Lakes brought a general half- to one-foot snowfall across the northern sections of the region. Along the south shore of Lake Ontario amounts were enhanced by additional moisture from the lake and totals ranged from a foot to a foot and a half. Many schools were closed on Friday the 8th.  The snowfall made for a slow, messy commute for the Buffalo, Niagara Falls and Rochester metro areas.  Specific snowfall totals included: 18 inches at Gasport; 17 inches at Lockport; 16 inches at Irondequoit, Greece, and Sandborn; 15 inches at Grand Island, Henderson, and Spencerport; 14 inches at Lewiston, Holley, Newark and Youngstown; 13 inches at West Carthage, Niagara Falls, Marion and Walworth; 12 inches at Tonawanda, Lowville, Constableville, and Rochester Airport; 11 inches at Fulton, Oakfield and Lyndonville; 10 inches at Swormville, Kenmore, and Highmarket; 9 inches at Alden; 8 inches at Montezuma and Buffalo airport; and 7 inches at Redfield.

Lake snows persisted as a deep trough of low pressure became entrenched across the northeast United States. |Off Lake Erie, a band of lake effect snow developed along the Lake Erie shoreline Monday (21st) producing very heavy snow and difficult travel along the New York State Thruway corridor from Hamburg to the Pennsylvania State Line. From Tuesday through Thursday, multiple bands of somewhat lighter lake effect snow continued across much of the Southern Tier and occasionally drifted into southern Erie and Wyoming counties. The greatest totals off Lake Erie were found in southwest Chautauqua County and along the Chautauqua ridge where snow totals were near two feet. Periodic bands of somewhat weaker lake effect snow then continued along the south shore of the lake through midweek. The greatest totals off Lake Ontario were found over Oswego County ��� nearing two feet. About a foot fell along much of the south shore within a few miles of the lake from Eastern Niagara County to Wayne County.  Specific snowfall reports included:  22 inches at Palermo and Phoenix, 20 inches at Fulton, 18 inches at Fair Haven, 15 inches at Osceola, 13 inches at Lyndonville, 12 inches at Sodus, 11 inches at Hamlin Beach, 10 inches at Newfane.

A rapidly deepening storm system moved eastward from Lake Huron and tracked north of Lake Ontario to southwestern Quebec. The system was followed by a westerly flow of very cold air across the still unfrozen Great Lakes which triggered lake effect snows downwind of Lake Ontario. Off Lake Ontario, lake snows organized into a narrow but intense band of snow over southern Jefferson and Lewis Counties as winds aligned down the long axis of the lake. This band sagged south across with snowfall rates of up to 2 inches per hour were observed. The greatest snow totals off Lake Ontario fell during two periods. During the first, six to as much as twenty-one inches were measured in central Oswego and southern Lewis Counties.

A hot and very humid airmass was in place across western and north central New York with the region in a prolonged heat wave. Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours as a pre-frontal trough approached from the upper Great Lakes. The storms moved east across the region with winds along the gust front were measured to 60 mph and several estimated even high gusts where instrumentation was not available.  The strong winds downed trees and powers lines. Power outages, while scattered in nature were reported from a large portion of the area.  Roads were blocked by fallen trees and debris. Several reports of minor structural damage were also received.

An arctic airmass settled in over the region on January 22nd and remained in place for a few days as cold fronts moved across the region ushering in reinforcing surges of cold air. In addition to the cold air, winds remained rather brisk for much of the time resulting in dangerously cold wind chill values. Minimum temperatures dropped below zero across most east central New York each night and during the day temperatures only warmed into the single digits to teens.

A hot and very humid airmass was in place across western and north central New York with the region in a prolonged heat wave. Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours as a pre-frontal trough approached from the upper Great Lakes. The storms moved east across the region with winds along the gust front were measured to 60 mph and several estimated even high gusts where instrumentation was not available.  The strong winds downed trees and powers lines. Power outages, while scattered in nature were reported from a large portion of the area.  Roads were blocked by fallen trees and debris. Several reports of minor structural damage were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported trees downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold temperatures in late January caused a freeze up ice jam in Malone NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A freeze up ice jam developed on the Salmon River along Lower Park Street in Malone NY late on January 23. Water rose overnight, and a state of emergency was declared and homes were evacuated the morning of January 24. Seven homes had flooded basements or water into the first floor, and portions of Lower Park Street were inundated by several feet of water. Cold temperatures remained until the end of January, and the ice jam finally released on January 31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed in a warm, moist, unstable air mass as a cold front approached from the west.  The thunderstorms produced damaging winds. Hail up to one-inch in diameter fell near Sherman. The thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines. In Cherry Creek and Leon, both in Cattaraugus County, several roads were blocked by fallen trees. In Springwater, in Livingston County, a house was damaged when thunderstorm winds knocked a tree onto it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down by thunderstorm winds.
