EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front passed through the region on Sunday, January 20th, 2013.  As this cold front passed by, much colder air aloft moved into the area.  As the cooling aloft combined with the heating of the sun in the wake of the front, the atmosphere became mixed and allowed strong, gusty winds to reach the surface.  Winds gusted to 60 mph across portions of the Mohawk Valley, southern Adirondacks, and Saratoga Region during the late morning and early afternoon hours.  This caused downed trees and power lines, as well as some power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 196 was reported closed in Kingsbury due to downed wires from high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures and rainfall beginning January 30 caused rivers to swell and the breakup of ice cover. Ice jams resulted on January 31, primarily on the Ausable River and its tributaries, and the Great Chazy River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam formed on the East Branch of the Ausable River between Keene and Jay, and moved downstream. Evacuations were necessary in Jay. The jam moved downstream through Ausable Forks and caused the East Branch Ausable River to spike above flood stage briefly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved through central New York Saturday afternoon, November 23rd. This front brought a very cold northwest flow of air to central New York. This led to significant lake effect snows across portions of central New York through the afternoon hours of November 24th. Snow amounts ranged from 7 to 12 inches with the highest total of 11.5 inches occurring in Sempronius, NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amount of 10 inches was reported in Skaneateles.

An area of low pressure passing over the lower Great Lakes brought a general half- to one-foot snowfall across the northern sections of the region. Along the south shore of Lake Ontario amounts were enhanced by additional moisture from the lake and totals ranged from a foot to a foot and a half. Many schools were closed on Friday the 8th.  The snowfall made for a slow, messy commute for the Buffalo, Niagara Falls and Rochester metro areas.  Specific snowfall totals included: 18 inches at Gasport; 17 inches at Lockport; 16 inches at Irondequoit, Greece, and Sandborn; 15 inches at Grand Island, Henderson, and Spencerport; 14 inches at Lewiston, Holley, Newark and Youngstown; 13 inches at West Carthage, Niagara Falls, Marion and Walworth; 12 inches at Tonawanda, Lowville, Constableville, and Rochester Airport; 11 inches at Fulton, Oakfield and Lyndonville; 10 inches at Swormville, Kenmore, and Highmarket; 9 inches at Alden; 8 inches at Montezuma and Buffalo airport; and 7 inches at Redfield.

A dual area of low pressure moved across New York and just south of New England on December 15th to bring the first widespread snowfall of the 2013-14 winter season. The coastal storm became the more dominate system and delivered 10 to 15 inches across southeast Vermont with 5 to 8 inches across northern New York. The typical impacts associated with this storm were the numerous vehicle accidents, especially being the first storm of the season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to eight inches of snow fell across Essex county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass settled in over the region on January 22nd and remained in place for a few days as cold fronts moved across the region ushering in reinforcing surges of cold air. In addition to the cold air, winds remained rather brisk for much of the time resulting in dangerously cold wind chill values. Minimum temperatures dropped below zero across most east central New York each night and during the day temperatures only warmed into the single digits to teens.

An arctic cold front moved through central New York Saturday afternoon, November 23rd. This front brought a very cold northwest flow of air to central New York. This led to significant lake effect snows across portions of central New York through the afternoon hours of November 24th. Snow amounts ranged from 7 to 12 inches with the highest total of 11.5 inches occurring in Sempronius, NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amount of 6.5 inches was recorded in Cortland.

Rapidly deepening low pressure tracked from the Upper Great Lakes to James Bay and brought strong winds to the entire region. The winds, gusting as high as 68 mph, brought down trees and power lines throughout the region with numerous reports of damage from downed trees. Power outages were in the tens of thousands. In Stafford, the winds tore a portion of a roof off a house. In Pulaski, a man was blown off a two-story building while installing a new metal roof. A gust of wind blew off the piece of metal roofing that he was hanging onto off the structure. He was transported to the hospital where he later died from his injuries. Other reports of damage were received from Angola, Dickersonville, Abion, Buffalo, Stafford, Carthage, Croghan, Newark, Victory, and Fulton. Specific measure wind gusts included: 68 mph at Dunkirk; 63 mph at Rochester Airport and Niagara Falls Airport; 61 mph at Barcelona; and 59 mph at Buffalo Airport.

An area of low pressure moved across the Ohio Valley towards western New York on Saturday, December 14th.  At the same time, another area of low pressure began to develop over the mid-Atlantic coast late in the day and rapidly strengthened overnight as it moved up the eastern seaboard.  This storm was located off the coast of New England during the day on Sunday, December 15th.  ||As a result, snow slowly spread from southwest to northeast during the day on December 14th.  With a cold and dry airmass in place, the snow was initially light in intensity and little accumulation occurred during the day.  ||However, as the secondary low began to rapidly develop, a steadier and heavier snowfall moved across the region during the evening hours and into the overnight.  Snow fell at rates in excess of one inch per hour over much of the region and snow rates locally were as high as up to three inches per hour at times.  During the overnight hours, sleet and freezing rain mixed in with the snow across portions of the mid-Hudson Valley and Taconics, but the precipitation remained all in the form of snow north and west of there. ||The bulk of the accumulating snow was finished by the late overnight hours, but light snow showers and flurries continued into the mid-morning hours, especially across the Adirondacks and Capital Region.  By the end of the storm, many places around the eastern Catskills, Capital Region and Lake George Saratoga Region received around a foot of snowfall. Lighter snowfall of 5 to 8 inches occured in the mid-Hudson Valley due to the mixing with sleet and freezing rain. Lighter snowfall amounts of 6 to 9 inches occurred in the western Adirondacks and western Mohawk Valley, as the heaviest snow remained east of these locations.

A strengthening area of low pressure moved north along the U.S. East Coast. The storm brought accumulating now across western New York. Across parts of the North Country, the snow mixed with sleet and freezing rain. Although not exceptionally high snowfall totals, strong winds accompanying the system resulted in a considerable amount of blowing snow resulting in frequent white out conditions. Several counties issued travel advisories due to the hazardous road conditions. The fact that the storm occurred just a day or two prior to Thanksgiving only added to the impact of the storm. Specific snowfall reports included: 9 inches at Perrysburg, Glenwood Warsaw; 8 inches at Randolph, Little Valley and Frewsburg; 7 inches at Rushford, Franklinville, Jamestown, Colden, Stafford, Pavilion, Greece and Macedon; 6 inches at Clayton, Geneseo and Youngstown; and 5 inches at Phelps and Medina.

A line of thunderstorms accompanied the passage of a strong cold front as it swept western New York. The thunderstorms produced wind gusts measured to 58 mph. The thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines. In Dunkirk, the storage door to a gas station garage was blown off. In Fredonia, an uprooted tree fell on an apartment building.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of trees down in Westfield were received from the general public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of lake effect snow off Lake Ontario affected portions of the western Adirondacks on the late afternoon and evening hours of Tuesday, December 31st.  The band of lake-effect snow produced snow rates in excess of two inches per hour at times, leading to very low visibility and difficult travel conditions across northern Herkimer County. The band of snow began to weaken and shift away from the area by the late evening hours.  By the time the snow ended, over 9 inches of snow fell in the Old Forge area.
A passing cold front triggered isolated severe thunderstorms that moved across portions of southeast New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Route 9W was closed due to downed trees and wires near Corbin Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across the Ohio Valley towards western New York on Saturday, December 14th.  At the same time, another area of low pressure began to develop over the mid-Atlantic coast late in the day and rapidly strengthened overnight as it moved up the eastern seaboard.  This storm was located off the coast of New England during the day on Sunday, December 15th.  ||As a result, snow slowly spread from southwest to northeast during the day on December 14th.  With a cold and dry airmass in place, the snow was initially light in intensity and little accumulation occurred during the day.  ||However, as the secondary low began to rapidly develop, a steadier and heavier snowfall moved across the region during the evening hours and into the overnight.  Snow fell at rates in excess of one inch per hour over much of the region and snow rates locally were as high as up to three inches per hour at times.  During the overnight hours, sleet and freezing rain mixed in with the snow across portions of the mid-Hudson Valley and Taconics, but the precipitation remained all in the form of snow north and west of there. ||The bulk of the accumulating snow was finished by the late overnight hours, but light snow showers and flurries continued into the mid-morning hours, especially across the Adirondacks and Capital Region.  By the end of the storm, many places around the eastern Catskills, Capital Region and Lake George Saratoga Region received around a foot of snowfall. Lighter snowfall of 5 to 8 inches occured in the mid-Hudson Valley due to the mixing with sleet and freezing rain. Lighter snowfall amounts of 6 to 9 inches occurred in the western Adirondacks and western Mohawk Valley, as the heaviest snow remained east of these locations.

A cold front swept from north to south on Sunday, leading to cold northwest flow over the Great Lakes. This generated lake effect snow for central New York. Snowfall amounts ranged from 7 to 13 inches for cities along and north of the New York Thruway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow produced 7 to 9 inches. The highest snowfall total of 12.8 inches occurred 4 miles north-northwest of Durhamville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A westerly flow of cold air over the Great Lakes produced significant lake effect snow over Oneida county in central New York. Snowfall amounts ranged from 7 to 18 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapidly deepening low pressure tracked from the Upper Great Lakes to James Bay and brought strong winds to the entire region. The winds, gusting as high as 68 mph, brought down trees and power lines throughout the region with numerous reports of damage from downed trees. Power outages were in the tens of thousands. In Stafford, the winds tore a portion of a roof off a house. In Pulaski, a man was blown off a two-story building while installing a new metal roof. A gust of wind blew off the piece of metal roofing that he was hanging onto off the structure. He was transported to the hospital where he later died from his injuries. Other reports of damage were received from Angola, Dickersonville, Abion, Buffalo, Stafford, Carthage, Croghan, Newark, Victory, and Fulton. Specific measure wind gusts included: 68 mph at Dunkirk; 63 mph at Rochester Airport and Niagara Falls Airport; 61 mph at Barcelona; and 59 mph at Buffalo Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and deepening area of low pressure moved across Ontario and Quebec provinces on November 1st. The associated cold front moved across northern New York during the morning to early afternoon hours and delivered strong southwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts in excess of 60 mph. These winds caused numerous reports of tree branches and trees down as well as on utility lines that caused power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered reports of downed tree branches, trees on roads and utility lines in Clinton county, including Ellenburg, Churubusco.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coupled area of low pressure traveled north along a stationary front across eastern New York during the night of November 26th and continuing through the 27th. The atmosphere was marginally cold enough for 6 to 10 inches of a heavy, wet snow across the St. Lawrence River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy, wet snow accumulated 5 to 9 inches across the region, mixing with and changing to sleet and freezing rain multiple times during the event.

A dual area of low pressure, one across the Great Lakes and the other moving offshore of southern New England, on February 19th delivered a moderate snowfall to portions of northern New York. Snow began just after midnight on the 19th and ended by late evening. Total snowfall accumulations ranged from 3 to 5 inches in St. Lawrence county to 8 to 12 inches across Essex and Clinton counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to six inches of snow fell across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snows developed off both Lake Erie and Ontario in a typical westerly flow.  Snowfall rates of an inch an hour off Lake Erie and two inches an hour off Lake Ontario were reported. Diurnal effect diminished the lake snows somewhat during the day and dry air building in from the central Great Lakes brought an end to the snows by the late evening of the 21st. Specific snowfall reports off Lake Erie:  20 inches at Westfield, Springville and Franklinville; 19 inches at Perrysburg and 15 inches at Ripley. Snowfall reports off Lake Ontario:  36 inches at North Osceola; 26 inches at Constableville; 25 inches at Bennetts Bridge; and 21 inches at Hooker.

A storm system rapidly intensified across the Great Lakes on January 30th and moved into Quebec province during January 31st. Mild southerly winds on January 30th translated to colder west to northwest winds across northern New York on January 31st. Sustained winds of 15 to 25-30 mph were observed with several gusts in excess of 40 mph and a few measured gusts exceeding 50 mph.  These strong winds knocked down several tree limbs and a few trees for scattered power outages across northern New York, especially Essex ad Clinton counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 45 mph was recorded in Essex county with some damage but winds estimated slightly less than Clinton county.

Diurnal effect diminished the lake snows somewhat during the day and dry air building in from the central Great Lakes brought an end to the snows by the late evening of the 21st. Specific snowfall reports off Lake Erie:  20 inches at Westfield, Springville and Franklinville; 19 inches at Perrysburg and 15 inches at Ripley. Snowfall reports off Lake Ontario:  36 inches at North Osceola; 26 inches at Constableville; 25 inches at Bennetts Bridge; and 21 inches at Hooker.

A storm system rapidly intensified across the Great Lakes on January 30th and moved into Quebec province during January 31st. Mild southerly winds on January 30th translated to colder west to northwest winds across northern New York on January 31st. Sustained winds of 15 to 25-30 mph were observed with several gusts in excess of 40 mph and a few measured gusts exceeding 50 mph.  These strong winds knocked down several tree limbs and a few trees for scattered power outages across northern New York, especially Essex ad Clinton counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 51 mph was recorded at Saranac Lake airport with several reports of tree, tree limb and power line damage in Franklin county suggesting potentially 50 knot wind gusts in some locales.

A warm frontal boundary lifted north and stalled across the lower Great Lakes which lead to a period of significant rainfall across the region between the 9th and 12th. The rainfall pushed many area streams and creeks in western New York above action stage. Black Creek at Churchville exceeded flood stage (6 feet) for around 21 hours, cresting at 6.37 feet. This resulted some backyard and basement flooding in Churchville and Chili.

A large ridge of high pressure was situated along the eastern seaboard for the week of July 15th through July 19th.  The flow around this high pressure ridge brought a very warm and humid air mass into eastern New York for much of the week, along with plenty of sunshine.  ||The result was high temperatures each day exceeding 90 degrees.  When combined with the humidity, heat index values exceeded 100 degrees at times during the afternoon and evening hours, mainly for the mid Hudson Valley. The hottest days were July 18th-19th, when high temperatures reached the mid to upper 90s in many locations.  On the 19th, heat index values were between 105 and 110 degrees across portions of Ulster and Dutchess Counties, with heat index values over 100 degrees across much of eastern New York.  The New York State Independent System Operator (NYISO) reported that power consumption reached a record high on Friday, July 19th, exceeding the record set in 2006. ||By the weekend, an approaching cold front brought somewhat cooler and less humid air into the region, which allowed heat index values to be lower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front crossed central New York State during the late afternoon and early evening hours of Friday April 19th. This front was accompanied by a squall line which produced locally damaging wind including a confirmed EF1 Tornado in Chenango and Delaware counties. Most areas saw brief heavy rain and wind gusts to 50 mph with a few locations seeing intense straight line winds between 80 and 110 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down about 4 miles South Southeast of Bainbridge in Chenango County and tracked northeast about 3.2 miles, and then ended about 3 miles South of Sidney in Delaware County.||The tornado touched down between Interstate 88 and highway 206, on East Afton Road, where 2 power poles were snapped and a car was destroyed by a large tree branch. Another vehicle was also damaged. Several trees were snapped or uprooted. A barn had minor roof damage. The tornado continued northeast and crossed highway 206, where a farm equipment building and a mobile home were heavily damaged, and a storage trailer was destroyed. Other nearby homes had minor damage, and a carport was lifted and moved 75 feet. Farther down its path, the tornado intensified to a high end EF1 strength while traveling in the vicinity of Houck Drive. In this area, it uprooted or snapped many 1 to 2 foot diameter trees along its path, both hardwoods, maples, and softwoods, pines, with the largest more than 3 feet wide. It also caused structural damage to a single family residence and garage, while transporting a significant amount of debris a hundred yards or more. The tornado then crossed into Delaware county, downing several more trees on some properties along Highway 8 before dissipating.

After several weeks of very wet weather, the ground across the region was already quite saturated due to recent flooding.  This made the region susceptible to additional flooding.||An unusually strong area of low pressure for late June moved from central Pennsylvania northeastward across eastern New York during the early morning hours of Friday, June 28th.   This storm system was more typical of a winter-like storm, due to deep low pressure and heavy precipitation spiraling around the western side of the storm system.  A strong southerly flow ahead of this system transported very moist air into the region.||Heavy rainfall moved across the Mohawk Valley and western Adirondacks in advance of the low pressure during the early morning hours. The rain fell heaviest during the late night hours before tapering off during the late morning.  Rainfall fell at rates of nearly one inch per hour at times with three to five inches of rainfall having occurred in total. This led to significant flash flooding across both the Mohawk Valley and Adirondacks. ||Many roads were washed out and closed, including a portion of the New York State Thruway between exits 29 (Canajoharie) and 29A (Little Falls).  In addition, urbanized areas along the Mohawk River experienced dangerous amounts of flooding.  The entire town of Fort Plain was under water and many swift water rescues had to take place.  Many homes and trailers were impacted by flood waters.  The New York State Barge Canal was closed as a result of the flooding.  In addition, one fatality occurred as a result of the flooding. ||State of emergencies were declared in many communities as a result of the flooding including:  Herkimer County, Montgomery County, Village of Ilion (Herkimer County),  Town and Village of Herkimer (Herkimer County), Village of Mohawk (Herkimer County), Town of Johnsburg (Warren County), Town of Minden (Montgomery County), and the Town of German Flatts (Herkimer County). | |On July 12th, President Obama signed a New York Disaster Declaration and ordered federal aid to supplement state and local recovery efforts in the area affected by severe storms and flooding during the period of June 28 to July 4, 2013. This included Herkimer, Montgomery and Warren counties in east central New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding occurred throughout Canajoharie due to flash flooding from heavy rainfall, especially for areas along the Canajoharie Creek.  Interstate 90 (The New York State Thruway) was closed between exits 29 and 29A due to flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gage at Kast Bridge recorded flash flooding as a result of heavy rainfall. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and moist air mass remained in place over the region on June 30th.  At the surface, a weak stationary front was draped across the area.  During the afternoon hours, a weak upper level disturbance moved across the region.  This allowed some showers and thunderstorms to develop in the vicinity of the surface front.  With the moist air mass in place, these showers and thunderstorms produced locally heavy rainfall.  With the ground already saturated due to several weeks of wet weather, this led to flash flooding in a few locations.  In addition, some of the thunderstorms became strong enough to produce wind damage to trees and large hail.  The showers and thunderstorm activity waned by evening, as the disturbance moved east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported in Manheim Center during a thunderstorm.

On the morning of Thursday, July 11th, a beaver dam broke along Foster Brook in Huletts Landing in Washington County.  Newspaper media reported that recent heavy rains and a persistent waterfall caused the sudden release of the dam.  A pond above the dam was released and this allowed a large amount of water to rush down the mountainside brook and flood portions of Huletts Landing.  The flood waters affected several homes and washed over County Route 6A.  A large amount of debris, including boulders and wood, were washed into Lake George.  No structural damage or injuries were reported from the flooding. The water receded about two hours after the dam break occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the morning of Thursday, July 11th, a beaver dam broke along Foster Brook in Huletts Landing in Washington County. Newspaper media reported that recent heavy rains and a persistent waterfall caused the sudden release of the dam. A pond above the dam was released and this allowed a large amount of water to rush down the mountainside brook and flood portions of Huletts Landing. The flood waters affected several homes and washed over County Route 6A. No structural damage or injuries were reported from the flooding. A large amount of debris, including boulders and wood, were washed into Lake George. The water receded about two hours after the dam break occurred.

During the afternoon hours, a weak upper level disturbance moved across the region.  This allowed some showers and thunderstorms to develop in the vicinity of the surface front.  With the moist air mass in place, these showers and thunderstorms produced locally heavy rainfall.  With the ground already saturated due to several weeks of wet weather, this led to flash flooding in a few locations.  In addition, some of the thunderstorms became strong enough to produce wind damage to trees and large hail.  The showers and thunderstorm activity waned by evening, as the disturbance moved east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several small trees were downed as a result of thunderstorm winds near Little Falls. The trees blocked Burrell Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm moved on-shore from Lake Ontario and crossed Orleans and Monroe counties. The thunderstorm produced hail which reached one inch in diameter near Albion. At peak, the hail covered the ground.

An isolated thunderstorm moved on-shore from Lake Ontario and crossed Orleans and Monroe counties. The thunderstorm produced hail which reached one inch in diameter near Albion. At peak, the hail covered the ground.

A large ridge of high pressure was situated along the eastern seaboard for the week of July 15th through July 19th.  The flow around this high pressure ridge brought a very warm and humid air mass into eastern New York for much of the week, along with plenty of sunshine.  ||The result was high temperatures each day exceeding 90 degrees.  When combined with the humidity, heat index values exceeded 100 degrees at times during the afternoon and evening hours, mainly for the mid Hudson Valley. The hottest days were July 18th-19th, when high temperatures reached the mid to upper 90s in many locations.  On the 19th, heat index values were between 105 and 110 degrees across portions of Ulster and Dutchess Counties, with heat index values over 100 degrees across much of eastern New York.  The New York State Independent System Operator (NYISO) reported that power consumption reached a record high on Friday, July 19th, exceeding the record set in 2006. ||By the weekend, an approaching cold front brought somewhat cooler and less humid air into the region, which allowed heat index values to be lower.

On the evening of Monday, July 22nd a warm front was lifting northward from New Jersey into southern New York.  Ahead of this warm front, a steady light to moderate rain was in place across the Catskills and Hudson Valley region.  Embedded within this steady rain were pocket of heavy rainfall and thunderstorms moving into a northward direction.  These pockets of heavy rainfall and thunderstorms moved over the same locations across the mid-Hudson Valley, as the warm front slowly lifted northward.  As a result, very heavy rainfall repeatedly fell over the same locations over a several hour period.  The result was flash flooding across parts of eastern Greene County.  Radar estimates and spotters reported 4 to 7 inches of rain across the region.  Several roadways were closed as a result of the flooding and flood waters even covered a portion of the New York State Thruway.  The water receded by the early morning hours of Tuesday, July 23rd, as the rainfall tapered off across the region, as the warm front continued to lift northward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 9W (West Bridge Street) in Catskill was closed due to flash flooding from heavy rainfall. One foot of water was flowing across the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front crossed central New York State during the late afternoon and early evening hours of Friday April 19th. This front was accompanied by a squall line which produced locally damaging wind including a confirmed EF1 Tornado in Chenango and Delaware counties. Most areas saw brief heavy rain and wind gusts to 50 mph with a few locations seeing intense straight line winds between 80 and 110 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down about 4 miles South Southeast of Bainbridge in Chenango County and tracked northeast about 3.2 miles, and then ended about 3 miles South of Sidney in Delaware County.||The tornado touched down between Interstate 88 and highway 206, on East Afton Road, where 2 power poles were snapped and a car was destroyed by a large tree branch. Another vehicle was also damaged. Several trees were snapped or uprooted. A barn had minor roof damage. The tornado continued northeast and crossed highway 206, where a farm equipment building and a mobile home were heavily damaged, and a storage trailer was destroyed. Other nearby homes had minor damage, and a carport was lifted and moved 75 feet. Farther down its path, the tornado intensified to a high end EF1 strength while traveling in the vicinity of Houck Drive. In this area, it uprooted or snapped many 1 to 2 foot diameter trees along its path, both hardwoods, maples, and softwoods, pines, with the largest more than 3 feet wide. It also caused structural damage to a single family residence and garage, while transporting a significant amount of debris a hundred yards or more. The tornado then crossed into Delaware county, downing several more trees on some properties along Highway 8 before dissipating.

An unusually moist atmosphere, even for early August standards, was in place across eastern New York on Friday, August 9th. A cold front, aided by strong upper level dynamics, was approaching the region from the Great Lakes.||Ahead of this front, a large area of heavy rainfall and embedded thunderstorms moved across the region during the morning and afternoon hours. Areas of heavy rainfall repeatedly moved across the same portions of the mid-Hudson Valley. This heavy rainfall of more than five inches in spots caused streams and creeks to overflow their banks, resulting in flash flooding. Roads were closed as a result of the flooding and people had to be rescued due to water stranding vehicles. ||In addition, one of thunderstorms embedded within the heavy rainfall contained gusty winds.  With the saturated soils, this caused several trees to fall in Beacon Hills.||By the evening hours, the heavy rain had ended and the water begun to recede.  Much less humid air moved into the region by that night as the cold front swept through the area, ending the threat for severe weather and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A creek was overflowing its banks as a result of heavy rainfall in Lagrangeville. Water of 6 to 12 inches deep was flowing across the roadway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Belt Parkway was impassable near exit 6 in Coney Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed in tropical moisture feeding along a stationary boundary. Record rainfall in May and June saturated the ground and elevated water levels in rivers and streams, making the region vulnerable to flooding. Some storms produced over two inches of rain in one to two hours, resulting in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding closed Route 9 and 22, and washed out portions of Highland Road in northeast Essex County NY. A northbound Amtrak train was halted on fears of a track washout near its crossing with Route 22 in Willsboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rainfall developed on 6/29 in a warm unstable airmass. The region had been hit with excessive rainfall during the preceding month, and the ground was saturated and rivers and streams were running high.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall over the higher terrain of central Clinton County NY caused flooding of Military Turnpike (Route 190), closing it from Duley Road to Seymour Road.

A very warm and humid air mass was in place over eastern New York on Friday, July 19th. With temperatures well into the 90s, the air mass was very unstable.  ||A cold front was upstream of the region across the Great Lakes.  Ahead of this front, a complex of thunderstorms developed across Lake Ontario during the evening hours of July 19th.  As these thunderstorms moved eastward, they strengthened as they interacted with a lake-breeze boundary over central New York.  The thunderstorms raced eastward across the Mohawk Valley, southern Adirondacks, and Saratoga Region during the late evening hours.  Due to the quick movement of the storms and the very unstable air mass in place, the thunderstorms mainly produced damaging winds, although there was also a report of large hail and heavy rainfall, which led to a mudslide in Herkimer County.||The thunderstorms moved east of the region by midnight, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down on Rock City Road in Ballston Spa as a result of thunderstorm winds.

On the 19th, heat index values were between 105 and 110 degrees across portions of Ulster and Dutchess Counties, with heat index values over 100 degrees across much of eastern New York.  The New York State Independent System Operator (NYISO) reported that power consumption reached a record high on Friday, July 19th, exceeding the record set in 2006. ||By the weekend, an approaching cold front brought somewhat cooler and less humid air into the region, which allowed heat index values to be lower.

The region was sandwiched between a strong stalled upper level low over the Ohio Valley and a ridge of high pressure situated over the western Atlantic Ocean.  The combined flow around both of these feature allowed a moist southerly flow directly out of the Caribbean Sea to move up the eastern seaboard.   Showers and thunderstorms developed along a very slow moving surface boundary and the persistent southerly flow caused a lot of these showers and thunderstorms to develop and pass over the same areas.  ||Due to heavy rainfall over the last month, the ground was rather saturated and it didn't take much additional rain to cause issues.  As a result, flash flooding occurred throughout the day on Monday, July 1st and again during the evening on Tuesday, July 2nd.  Roads were damaged and closed and some evacuations took place as homes were impacted by the flood waters.  The hardest hit areas were across part of the Mohawk Valley, where significant flash flooding had occurred just a few days prior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fitch Road in Saratoga Lake was washed out and closed due to flash flooding from heavy rainfall. Houses at the intersection of Route 9P and Fitch Road were impacted by the flood waters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The westbound Staten Island Expressway was closed at Renwick Ave. due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough ahead of a cold front triggered numerous showers and thunderstorms. These storms produced heavy rain, which resulted in flash flooding across portions of The Lower Hudson Valley and New York City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding in a home on Bramble Brook Rd. in Ardsley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually moist atmosphere, even for early August standards, was in place across eastern New York on Friday, August 9th. A cold front, aided by strong upper level dynamics, was approaching the region from the Great Lakes.||Ahead of this front, a large area of heavy rainfall and embedded thunderstorms moved across the region during the morning and afternoon hours. Areas of heavy rainfall repeatedly moved across the same portions of the mid-Hudson Valley. This heavy rainfall of more than five inches in spots caused streams and creeks to overflow their banks, resulting in flash flooding. Roads were closed as a result of the flooding and people had to be rescued due to water stranding vehicles. ||In addition, one of thunderstorms embedded within the heavy rainfall contained gusty winds.  With the saturated soils, this caused several trees to fall in Beacon Hills.||By the evening hours, the heavy rain had ended and the water begun to recede.  Much less humid air moved into the region by that night as the cold front swept through the area, ending the threat for severe weather and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed in Wappingers Falls as a result of flash flooding from heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid air mass was in place over eastern New York on Friday, July 19th. With temperatures well into the 90s, the air mass was very unstable.  ||A cold front was upstream of the region across the Great Lakes.  Ahead of this front, a complex of thunderstorms developed across Lake Ontario during the evening hours of July 19th.  As these thunderstorms moved eastward, they strengthened as they interacted with a lake-breeze boundary over central New York.  The thunderstorms raced eastward across the Mohawk Valley, southern Adirondacks, and Saratoga Region during the late evening hours.  Due to the quick movement of the storms and the very unstable air mass in place, the thunderstorms mainly produced damaging winds, although there was also a report of large hail and heavy rainfall, which led to a mudslide in Herkimer County.||The thunderstorms moved east of the region by midnight, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were reported down on East High Street in Ballston Spa as a result of thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and moist air mass remained in place over the region on June 30th.  At the surface, a weak stationary front was draped across the area.  During the afternoon hours, a weak upper level disturbance moved across the region.  This allowed some showers and thunderstorms to develop in the vicinity of the surface front.  With the moist air mass in place, these showers and thunderstorms produced locally heavy rainfall.  With the ground already saturated due to several weeks of wet weather, this led to flash flooding in a few locations.  In addition, some of the thunderstorms became strong enough to produce wind damage to trees and large hail.  The showers and thunderstorm activity waned by evening, as the disturbance moved east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were downed as a result of thunderstorm winds in Manheim Center.  A barn was damaged, a chicken coop demolished and three hay wagons were knocked over. In addition, a home sustained damage to it's siding as a result of the winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region remained in a warm and humid air mass as an upper level ridge remained just south and east across the western Atlantic Ocean. At the same time, a weak upper level disturbance passed by to the north across southern Canada.  This upper level disturbance allowed showers and thunderstorms to develop across the region during the afternoon and early evening hours of Monday, June 24th.  With a moist and unstable atmosphere in place, some of these storms became severe, with large hail and damaging wind gusts.  Thunderstorms dissipated by later in the evening, as the disturbance moved east of the region and the atmosphere stabilized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were down as a result of thunderstorm winds near Pleasant Plains in the Town of Clinton.  The result of the downed trees closed State Highway 9G for a brief time.

Several trees fell on homes, causing minor structural damage.  One of trees fell on a porch resulting in minor injuries to a man.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rainfall developed on 6/29 in a warm unstable airmass. The region had been hit with excessive rainfall during the preceding month, and the ground was saturated and rivers and streams were running high.

Tributaries to the Saranac River flooded local roads and highways, including General Leroy Manor Road, Route 374 in Cadyville between Trudeau Road and Church Road, as well as other local roads in the Rand Hill area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ridge of high pressure was situated along the eastern seaboard for the week of July 15th through July 19th.  The flow around this high pressure ridge brought a very warm and humid air mass into eastern New York for much of the week, along with plenty of sunshine.  ||The result was high temperatures each day exceeding 90 degrees.  When combined with the humidity, heat index values exceeded 100 degrees at times during the afternoon and evening hours, mainly for the mid Hudson Valley. The hottest days were July 18th-19th, when high temperatures reached the mid to upper 90s in many locations.  On the 19th, heat index values were between 105 and 110 degrees across portions of Ulster and Dutchess Counties, with heat index values over 100 degrees across much of eastern New York.  The New York State Independent System Operator (NYISO) reported that power consumption reached a record high on Friday, July 19th, exceeding the record set in 2006. ||By the weekend, an approaching cold front brought somewhat cooler and less humid air into the region, which allowed heat index values to be lower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved out of Pennsylvania and into central New York on June 27 and 28. Thunderstorms with heavy rainfall developed on the 27th, and heavy rainfall continued into the 28th across northern New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A stationary thunderstorm dumped over 3 inches of rain in a short time over the rugged terrain of central Essex County NY. Small creeks and streams left their banks and flooded local roads. Phelps Brook flooded Fox Run, Roscoe, and Cutting Roads, and Hurricane Mountain Road was washed out and closed. Moderate rain continued overnight, and the flash flood event transitioned to a more generalized flood event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved out of Pennsylvania and into central New York on June 27 and 28. Thunderstorms with heavy rainfall developed on the 27th, and heavy rainfall continued into the 28th across northern New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued across central Essex County New York as a flash flooding transitioned into a flood event. Areas were flooded as water traveled down stream on the East Branch Ausable and Boquet Rivers. In the Boquet Basin, Route 9 was flooded and closed in New Russia, and mobile homes beside the river were evacuated in Elizabethtown.  Beede Road in Keene Valley and portions of Route 9N were flooded between Keene Valley and Jay by the East Branch Ausable. The East Branch of the Ausable River at Ausable Forks NY (ASFN6) crested at moderate flood stage of 9.65 feet at 19:45 UTC on June 28.

The region remained in a warm and humid air mass as an upper level ridge remained just south and east across the western Atlantic Ocean. At the same time, a weak upper level disturbance passed by to the north across southern Canada.  This upper level disturbance allowed showers and thunderstorms to develop across the region during the afternoon and early evening hours of Monday, June 24th.  With a moist and unstable atmosphere in place, some of these storms became severe, with large hail and damaging wind gusts.  Thunderstorms dissipated by later in the evening, as the disturbance moved east of the region and the atmosphere stabilized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported toppled by thunderstorm winds on Steers Road in Perth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front and associated pre frontal trough approached upstate New York during the afternoon hours on June 17th, 2013.  With temperatures into the 80s and high levels of moisture in place, there was plenty of instability available for thunderstorms to develop.  A line of thunderstorms developed across the Capital Region and strong winds aloft aided in allowing these storms to become severe with damaging winds and large hail.  By later in the afternoon, the thunderstorms tracked east and away from the region, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported uprooted and large limbs down along State Route 155 in Guilderland due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and moist air mass remained in place over the region on June 30th.  At the surface, a weak stationary front was draped across the area.  During the afternoon hours, a weak upper level disturbance moved across the region.  This allowed some showers and thunderstorms to develop in the vicinity of the surface front.  With the moist air mass in place, these showers and thunderstorms produced locally heavy rainfall.  With the ground already saturated due to several weeks of wet weather, this led to flash flooding in a few locations.  In addition, some of the thunderstorms became strong enough to produce wind damage to trees and large hail.  The showers and thunderstorm activity waned by evening, as the disturbance moved east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported during a thunderstorm 1 mile south of Highland.

After several weeks of very wet weather, the ground across the region was already quite saturated due to recent flooding.  This made the region susceptible to additional flooding.||An unusually strong area of low pressure for late June moved from central Pennsylvania northeastward across eastern New York during the early morning hours of Friday, June 28th.   This storm system was more typical of a winter-like storm, due to deep low pressure and heavy precipitation spiraling around the western side of the storm system.  A strong southerly flow ahead of this system transported very moist air into the region.||Heavy rainfall moved across the Mohawk Valley and western Adirondacks in advance of the low pressure during the early morning hours. The rain fell heaviest during the late night hours before tapering off during the late morning.  Rainfall fell at rates of nearly one inch per hour at times with three to five inches of rainfall having occurred in total. This led to significant flash flooding across both the Mohawk Valley and Adirondacks. ||Many roads were washed out and closed, including a portion of the New York State Thruway between exits 29 (Canajoharie) and 29A (Little Falls).  In addition, urbanized areas along the Mohawk River experienced dangerous amounts of flooding.  The entire town of Fort Plain was under water and many swift water rescues had to take place.  Many homes and trailers were impacted by flood waters.  The New York State Barge Canal was closed as a result of the flooding.  In addition, one fatality occurred as a result of the flooding. ||State of emergencies were declared in many communities as a result of the flooding including:  Herkimer County, Montgomery County, Village of Ilion (Herkimer County),  Town and Village of Herkimer (Herkimer County), Village of Mohawk (Herkimer County), Town of Johnsburg (Warren County), Town of Minden (Montgomery County), and the Town of German Flatts (Herkimer County). | |On July 12th, President Obama signed a New York Disaster Declaration and ordered federal aid to supplement state and local recovery efforts in the area affected by severe storms and flooding during the period of June 28 to July 4, 2013. This included Herkimer, Montgomery and Warren counties in east central New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred throughout Ilion due to heavy rainfall. Water rescues and evacuations had to take place. Some roads were washed out and damaged due to the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front crossed central New York State during the late afternoon and early evening hours of Friday April 19th. This front was accompanied by a squall line which produced locally damaging wind including a confirmed EF1 Tornado in Chenango and Delaware counties. Most areas saw brief heavy rain and wind gusts to 50 mph with a few locations seeing intense straight line winds between 80 and 110 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A squall line with damaging winds blew down a power pole on building on county route 15.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ridge of high pressure was situated along the eastern seaboard for the week of July 15th through July 19th.  The flow around this high pressure ridge brought a very warm and humid air mass into eastern New York for much of the week, along with plenty of sunshine.  ||The result was high temperatures each day exceeding 90 degrees.  When combined with the humidity, heat index values exceeded 100 degrees at times during the afternoon and evening hours, mainly for the mid Hudson Valley. The hottest days were July 18th-19th, when high temperatures reached the mid to upper 90s in many locations.  On the 19th, heat index values were between 105 and 110 degrees across portions of Ulster and Dutchess Counties, with heat index values over 100 degrees across much of eastern New York.  The New York State Independent System Operator (NYISO) reported that power consumption reached a record high on Friday, July 19th, exceeding the record set in 2006. ||By the weekend, an approaching cold front brought somewhat cooler and less humid air into the region, which allowed heat index values to be lower.

A warm and humid air mass was in place across the region on the evening on Saturday, June 1st into the day on Sunday, June 2nd.  Daytime temperatures reached well into the 80s across much of the area. A cold front and associated pre-frontal surface trough was moving towards the area over the weekend. With plenty of moisture and instability in place, showers and thunderstorms developed across the region. These thunderstorms were aided by very strong winds aloft and a few storms became severe across eastern New York, producing wind damage and large hail. The thunderstorms weakened by evening as the cold front moved across the area, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down in West Winfield as a result of thunderstorm winds on State Route 51 and Burrows Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms containing torrential rainfall developed within a warm, humid and considerably unstable environment across upstate NY during the mid to late evening hours. Storms moving over the same locations produced areas of significant flash flooding throughout the central Leatherstocking region and the western Mohawk River valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Route 80 and Dugway Road were closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance allowed thunderstorms to develop during the evening hours. A storm tracking northeast out of northern Pennsylvania resulted in an EF1 tornado in Steuben County New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EF1 tornado had a 14 mile path length and 500 yard path width with estimated winds of 100 MPH. Numerous trees and power poles were snapped along its length. A barn was completely destroyed one mile southeast of Troupsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid air mass was in place across eastern New York on Wednesday, September 11th.  With temperatures into the 90s and dewpoints in the 70s, there was a large amount of instability across the region.  A stalled surface frontal boundary remained well north of the region across southern Canada.  ||As a subtle upper level disturbance moved across Pennsylvania, thunderstorms developed west of the region across central New York and moved eastward into eastern New York for the late afternoon and evening hours.  Some of these thunderstorms produced damaging winds to trees and power lines and large hail.  In addition, these thunderstorms produced a large amount of cloud to ground lightning, which caused damaged to trees and some structures.  A historic church in Ballston Center had a brief fire due to a lightning strike, which damaged the steeple.  Media reported that nearly 20,000 customers lost power as a result of the thunderstorms.  ||By late in the evening, thunderstorms weakened and moved east of the region, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down in Hartford as a result of thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, humid and unstable airmass resided over Central New York, and several upper level disturbances moving into the region triggered heavy rain producing showers and thunderstorms. These storms led to pockets of urban and small stream flash flooding in upstate New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused extensive urban and storm drain flash flooding throughout the City of Oneonta. Cars and people were stranded on local roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, humid and unstable airmass resided over Central New York, and several upper level disturbances moving into the region triggered heavy rain producing showers and thunderstorms. These storms led to pockets of urban and small stream flash flooding in upstate New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Area roads were severely flooded. Oneonta City Hall was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the day on Sunday, July 7th, a warm, southerly flow around a Bermuda High Pressure area allowed plenty of moisture to stream into eastern New York.  This allowed for an unstable air mass to be in place across the region.  As an upper level shortwave moved from the Ohio Valley towards the Northeast, scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours.  A few of these thunderstorms became severe, producing large hail and damaging winds.  Hail reached the size of golf balls across portions of Columbia County.  During one of the thunderstorms, a man was struck by lightning on a lake beach at a state park in Columbia County.  He went to cardiac arrest, and later died.||In addition, the very moist air mass allowed these thunderstorms to produce very heavy rainfall in a short period of time.  This led to flash flooding in a few locations, including portions of the Mohawk Valley that were still cleaning up from flash flooding earlier in the month and in late June. ||Thunderstorm activity diminished by the evening hours due to the loss of daytime heating, ending the threat for additional severe storms or flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported during a thunderstorm two miles west-northwest of Canaan.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were reported down across the town of Ardsley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple upper level shortwaves triggered pulse thunderstorms over the area, with isolated severe thunderstorms forming over the Lower Hudson River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees reported down along Broadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple thunderstorms formed along the sea breeze boundary across Long Island, with isolated severe mainly over the eastern end of the island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house caught on fire due to a lightning strike. Firefighters reported the roof had collapsed by the time of arrival. A dozen firefighters had to be treated for heat exhaustion fighting the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple thunderstorms formed along the sea breeze boundary across Long Island, with isolated severe mainly over the eastern end of the island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to quarter size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of cold front moved towards the region on Thursday, September 12th.  Despite some periods of cloudiness, a warm and humid air mass ahead of the approaching boundaries allowed for moderate amounts of instability to be in place.  Along and ahead of the boundaries, several lines of showers and thunderstorms developed and moved across the region during the afternoon and early evening hours.  In addition to a large amount of cloud to ground lightning, a few of the thunderstorms became severe, with damaging wind gusts.  Several trees were downed across the region.  Up to 4,600 people were without power at one point in the Capital Region due to the thunderstorms.||Some areas that received repeated showers and thunderstorms experienced flash flooding as well, with roads closed as a result.  The flooding even caused an evacuation of a senior hosing complex in Nassau.  Two to four inches of rain in a short period of time was reported in the areas that experienced flash flooding.||As the last in the series of cold fronts crossed during the evening hours, the threat for showers and thunderstorms ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was downed and landed on a deck on Dean Street in Old Niskayuna as a result of thunderstorm winds. Another small tree and some branches were reported down on Mohawk Trail in Niskayuna.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 26 year old man was struck by lightning on a beach at Lake Taghkanic State Park in Gallatin. The man was standing under a tree that was struck by lightning and the lightning traveled through this body.  He went into cardiac arrest, but was resuscitated at the park by emergency personnel.  He was then brought to a hospital in Hudson and transferred to Albany Medical Center where he later died.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a storm system, a southerly flow allowed for very warm and humid air to move into the region.  Temperatures were quite warm, especially considering that it was mid-September.   Under plenty of sunshine, temperatures warmed into the lower to middle 90s throughout the entire Hudson Valley, along with dewpoint values in the lower to middle 70s.  The allowed for heat index values to reach between 100 and 105 degrees.  The heat index values were highest between 2 pm and 5 pm.  Heat index values lowered by the evening hours as temperatures cooled.

Under plenty of sunshine, temperatures warmed into the lower to middle 90s throughout the entire Hudson Valley, along with dewpoint values in the lower to middle 70s. A very warm and humid air mass was in place across eastern New York on Wednesday, September 11th.  With temperatures into the 90s and dewpoints in the 70s, there was a large amount of instability across the region.  A stalled surface frontal boundary remained well north of the region across southern Canada.  ||As a subtle upper level disturbance moved across Pennsylvania, thunderstorms developed west of the region across central New York and moved eastward into eastern New York for the late afternoon and evening hours.  Some of these thunderstorms produced damaging winds to trees and power lines and large hail.  In addition, these thunderstorms produced a large amount of cloud to ground lightning, which caused damaged to trees and some structures.  A historic church in Ballston Center had a brief fire due to a lightning strike, which damaged the steeple.  Media reported that nearly 20,000 customers lost power as a result of the thunderstorms.  ||By late in the evening, thunderstorms weakened and moved east of the region, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down in Carlisle as a result of thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the day on Sunday, July 7th, a warm, southerly flow around a Bermuda High Pressure area allowed plenty of moisture to stream into eastern New York.  This allowed for an unstable air mass to be in place across the region.  As an upper level shortwave moved from the Ohio Valley towards the Northeast, scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours.  A few of these thunderstorms became severe, producing large hail and damaging winds.  Hail reached the size of golf balls across portions of Columbia County.  During one of the thunderstorms, a man was struck by lightning on a lake beach at a state park in Columbia County.  He went to cardiac arrest, and later died.||In addition, the very moist air mass allowed these thunderstorms to produce very heavy rainfall in a short period of time.  This led to flash flooding in a few locations, including portions of the Mohawk Valley that were still cleaning up from flash flooding earlier in the month and in late June. ||Thunderstorm activity diminished by the evening hours due to the loss of daytime heating, ending the threat for additional severe storms or flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were reported down due to thunderstorm winds in Ancram.

A series of cold front moved towards the region on Thursday, September 12th.  Despite some periods of cloudiness, a warm and humid air mass ahead of the approaching boundaries allowed for moderate amounts of instability to be in place.  Along and ahead of the boundaries, several lines of showers and thunderstorms developed and moved across the region during the afternoon and early evening hours.  In addition to a large amount of cloud to ground lightning, a few of the thunderstorms became severe, with damaging wind gusts.  Several trees were downed across the region.  Up to 4,600 people were without power at one point in the Capital Region due to the thunderstorms.||Some areas that received repeated showers and thunderstorms experienced flash flooding as well, with roads closed as a result.  The flooding even caused an evacuation of a senior hosing complex in Nassau.  Two to four inches of rain in a short period of time was reported in the areas that experienced flash flooding.||As the last in the series of cold fronts crossed during the evening hours, the threat for showers and thunderstorms ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down on Master Street, one mile north of the village of Valley Falls in the town of Schaghticoke, as a result of thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moving across Lake Ontario spawned a waterspout just north of Hilton, New York. The waterspout, caught on amateur video, came onshore and moved across a small section of Braddock Point before moving back out over water in Braddock Bay. Onshore, the tornado downed several large trees. A home on Ontario Boulevard and several automobiles were damaged by the falling trees.  The overall path length was approximated at less than a mile however the exact location of formation and dissipation over the water was unknown. Over land, the tornado path length was about a quarter of a mile, width was about ten yards, and it was ranked an EF0. Winds were estimated at 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout over Lake Ontario briefly crossed a small section of Braddock Point before moving back out over the waters of Braddock Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually moist atmosphere, even for early August standards, was in place across eastern New York on Friday, August 9th. A cold front, aided by strong upper level dynamics, was approaching the region from the Great Lakes.||Ahead of this front, a large area of heavy rainfall and embedded thunderstorms moved across the region during the morning and afternoon hours. Areas of heavy rainfall repeatedly moved across the same portions of the mid-Hudson Valley. This heavy rainfall of more than five inches in spots caused streams and creeks to overflow their banks, resulting in flash flooding. Roads were closed as a result of the flooding and people had to be rescued due to water stranding vehicles. ||In addition, one of thunderstorms embedded within the heavy rainfall contained gusty winds.  With the saturated soils, this caused several trees to fall in Beacon Hills.||By the evening hours, the heavy rain had ended and the water begun to recede.  Much less humid air moved into the region by that night as the cold front swept through the area, ending the threat for severe weather and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five trees were reported snapped or uprooted at a residence in Beacon Hills as a result of thunderstorm winds during heavy rainfall.

With temperatures into the 90s and dewpoints in the 70s, there was a large amount of instability across the region.  A stalled surface frontal boundary remained well north of the region across southern Canada.  ||As a subtle upper level disturbance moved across Pennsylvania, thunderstorms developed west of the region across central New York and moved eastward into eastern New York for the late afternoon and evening hours.  Some of these thunderstorms produced damaging winds to trees and power lines and large hail.  In addition, these thunderstorms produced a large amount of cloud to ground lightning, which caused damaged to trees and some structures.  A historic church in Ballston Center had a brief fire due to a lightning strike, which damaged the steeple.  Media reported that nearly 20,000 customers lost power as a result of the thunderstorms.  ||By late in the evening, thunderstorms weakened and moved east of the region, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported via Facebook an estimated thunderstorm wind gust of 60 MPH at the corner of Potter and West Mountain Roads in Queensbury.

A warm and very humid air mass was in place on the evening of Monday, July 8th. A few isolated heavy showers and thunderstorm developed and moved across eastern New York. One of these thunderstorms strengthened as it passed over the urbanized areas of Albany County and produced very heavy rainfall in a short period of time. This caused flash flooding in Colonie and Albany, including portions of the University at Albany campus. Flood waters quickly receded after thunderstorm activity ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported on the University at Albany campus as a result of heavy rainfall.  Some academic buildings were impacted by flood waters.  Cars in parking lots were stranded due to rising water levels as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid air mass was in place over eastern New York on Friday, July 19th. With temperatures well into the 90s, the air mass was very unstable.  ||A cold front was upstream of the region across the Great Lakes.  Ahead of this front, a complex of thunderstorms developed across Lake Ontario during the evening hours of July 19th.  As these thunderstorms moved eastward, they strengthened as they interacted with a lake-breeze boundary over central New York.  The thunderstorms raced eastward across the Mohawk Valley, southern Adirondacks, and Saratoga Region during the late evening hours.  Due to the quick movement of the storms and the very unstable air mass in place, the thunderstorms mainly produced damaging winds, although there was also a report of large hail and heavy rainfall, which led to a mudslide in Herkimer County.||The thunderstorms moved east of the region by midnight, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down via Facebook in Mayfield due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass was in place across eastern New York on Monday, September 2nd, Labor Day.  A southerly flow ahead of an approaching cold front brought plenty of moisture into the region from the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean.  This persistent southerly flow caused some showers and thunderstorms to develop over the region during the afternoon hours.  Some of these thunderstorms tracked over the same area, resulting in heavy rainfall in a short period of time across Dutchess County.  This caused flash flooding to occur in the Hyde Park area.||As the cold front approached by the late afternoon hours, the showers and thunderstorms organized into a squall line across the western Mohawk Valley. Hail and damaging winds occurred across several locations in southern Herkimer County.  At one point, up to 2,000 customers were without power in Herkimer County as a result of thunderstorms.  As the line of thunderstorms tracked eastward into the evening hours, they began to weaken, and the threat for severe weather ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding from heavy rainfall occurred in several locations on Crum Elbow Road in Hyde Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening storm system moved across the Upper Great lakes. The system brought strong, damaging winds to the entire region late Saturday night into Sunday (20th-21st). Trees, power poles and wires were brought down by the winds. Numerous roads were blocked by fallen trees, wires and debris. Some structural damage was also reported. Some of the more significant reported included: an awning ripped off in Lancaster, roof trusses were downed on an building under construction in Batavia, part of a metal roof blown off in Portageville, a trailer was damaged by a fallen tree in West Bloomfield, ladder assembly blown off a silo near Constableville, and a boat was destroyed by a fallen tree in Livonia. Utilities reported tens of thousands without power for a time. Specific measured gusts included:  77 mph at Oswego lake shore; 69 mph at Dunkirk; 66 mph at Olcott; 62 mph at Barcelona and Watertown; 61 mph at Buffalo Airport and Niagara Falls Airport; 59 mph at Jamestown, Rochester Airport, and Youngstown; 55 mph at Wellsville; 54 mph at Fort Drum; 52 mph at Fulton; and 51 mph at Livonia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported trees and wires down in Warsaw.

Trees, power poles and wires were brought down by the winds. Numerous roads were blocked by fallen trees, wires and debris. Some structural damage was also reported. Some of the more significant reported included: an awning ripped off in Lancaster, roof trusses were downed on an building under construction in Batavia, part of a metal roof blown off in Portageville, a trailer was damaged by a fallen tree in West Bloomfield, ladder assembly blown off a silo near Constableville, and a boat was destroyed by a fallen tree in Livonia. Utilities reported tens of thousands without power for a time. Specific measured gusts included:  77 mph at Oswego lake shore; 69 mph at Dunkirk; 66 mph at Olcott; 62 mph at Barcelona and Watertown; 61 mph at Buffalo Airport and Niagara Falls Airport; 59 mph at Jamestown, Rochester Airport, and Youngstown; 55 mph at Wellsville; 54 mph at Fort Drum; 52 mph at Fulton; and 51 mph at Livonia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees and wires down in Bristol.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved from the Great Lakes on the afternoon of Wednesday, January 30th towards the St. Lawrence Valley during the morning of Thursday, January 31st.  A strong cold front was associated with this area of low pressure.  Ahead of the storm, southerly winds allowed warm and moist air to move into the region.  While it took some time for the warmer air to overtake the cold, dense air in place, temperatures warmed into the 40s and 50s during the early morning hours of January 31st. ||Just ahead of the cold front, a line of heavy rain showers developed just prior to sunrise.  Strong wind gusts up to 60 mph accompanied some of this rainfall as it moved across eastern New York.  This caused damage to some trees and power lines, causing power outages throughout the region.  Saratoga County alone had nearly 4,500 National Grid Customers without power at one point.||With the rainfall falling heavy in intensity and warmer temperatures allowing for some snow melt, some ponding of water on roadways caused some isolated urban and street flooding. A few minor ice jams developed on small streams, but any flooding issues were very localized.||Behind the cold front, winds switched to a westerly direction and continued to be gusty throughout the day on Thursday, as colder temperatures moved into the region.  Additional wind gusts of 35 to 55 mph occurred during the daytime hours on Thursday, January 31st with some additional damage to trees and power lines.  Winds diminished by late Thursday afternoon as the pressure gradient began to relax over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that a fence was blown over due to high winds.  Numerous power outages were reported throughout Saratoga County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the Ohio Valley approached the region during the late evening of Tuesday, February 26th.  Snow, moderate to heavy at times in some areas, broke out over the region for the overnight hours.  ||Another area of low pressure developed over the mid-Atlantic region and moved northeast inland across the Northeast for the morning hours of Wednesday, February 27th.  The track of this low allowed warmer air, both at the surface and aloft, to move into the region.  This allowed the precipitation to change to rain and become spotty across the lower elevations, ending the snow accumulation for these areas. However, upslope flow allowed steady precipitation to continue across the higher terrain of the eastern Catskills, Helderbergs and southern Adirondacks through the day.  Although there was some mixing with sleet and freezing rain, the precipitation remained mainly snow for a long period of time across these higher terrain areas, allowing for moderate accumulations to occur.  The steady precipitation tapered off to snow showers during the early morning hours of Thursday, February 28th.||By the early morning of Thursday, February 28th, storm total snowfall amounts of 6 to 12 inches occurred across the higher terrain, while mainly just 1 to 6 inches occurred for valley areas.||In addition, east to southeast winds were gusty ahead of the approaching low pressure area during the morning hours of February 27th.  The strongest winds occurred across the higher terrain of the Taconics, where a wind gust was strong enough to blow over a tractor trailer truck in Copake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass settled in over the region on January 22nd and remained in place for a few days as cold fronts moved across the region ushering in reinforcing surges of cold air. In addition to the cold air, winds remained rather brisk for much of the time resulting in dangerously cold wind chill values. Minimum temperatures dropped below zero across most east central New York each night and during the day temperatures only warmed into the single digits to teens.

An intensifying low pressure system taking shape over the lower Mississippi Valley the morning of Saturday the 14th moved to a position south of Long Island by the morning of the 15th. This low brought heavy snow and some freezing rain to the upper boroughs of New York City northward into the Lower Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported widespread snowfall totals ranging from 7 to 8.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening storm system moved across the Upper Great lakes. The system brought strong, damaging winds to the entire region late Saturday night into Sunday (20th-21st). Trees, power poles and wires were brought down by the winds. Numerous roads were blocked by fallen trees, wires and debris. Some structural damage was also reported. Some of the more significant reported included: an awning ripped off in Lancaster, roof trusses were downed on an building under construction in Batavia, part of a metal roof blown off in Portageville, a trailer was damaged by a fallen tree in West Bloomfield, ladder assembly blown off a silo near Constableville, and a boat was destroyed by a fallen tree in Livonia. Utilities reported tens of thousands without power for a time. Specific measured gusts included:  77 mph at Oswego lake shore; 69 mph at Dunkirk; 66 mph at Olcott; 62 mph at Barcelona and Watertown; 61 mph at Buffalo Airport and Niagara Falls Airport; 59 mph at Jamestown, Rochester Airport, and Youngstown; 55 mph at Wellsville; 54 mph at Fort Drum; 52 mph at Fulton; and 51 mph at Livonia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees and wires down in Pembroke.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system rapidly intensified across the Great Lakes on January 30th and moved into Quebec province during January 31st. Mild southerly winds on January 30th translated to colder west to northwest winds across northern New York on January 31st. Sustained winds of 15 to 25-30 mph were observed with several gusts in excess of 40 mph and a few measured gusts exceeding 50 mph.  These strong winds knocked down several tree limbs and a few trees for scattered power outages across northern New York, especially Essex ad Clinton counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 44 mph was recorded in southern St. Lawrence county with several reports of tree, tree limb and power line damage across the region, thus suggesting wind gusts may have exceeded 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dissipating low pressure system across the Great Lakes and strengthening low pressure around the Delmarva peninsula accounted for a general 3 to 8 inch snowfall across northern New York on February 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A general 5 to 9 inches of snow fell across western Clinton county, while 3 to 6 inches fell in the Champlain Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved from the Great Lakes on the afternoon of Wednesday, January 30th towards the St. Lawrence Valley during the morning of Thursday, January 31st.  A strong cold front was associated with this area of low pressure.  Ahead of the storm, southerly winds allowed warm and moist air to move into the region.  While it took some time for the warmer air to overtake the cold, dense air in place, temperatures warmed into the 40s and 50s during the early morning hours of January 31st. ||Just ahead of the cold front, a line of heavy rain showers developed just prior to sunrise.  Strong wind gusts up to 60 mph accompanied some of this rainfall as it moved across eastern New York.  This caused damage to some trees and power lines, causing power outages throughout the region.  Saratoga County alone had nearly 4,500 National Grid Customers without power at one point.||With the rainfall falling heavy in intensity and warmer temperatures allowing for some snow melt, some ponding of water on roadways caused some isolated urban and street flooding. A few minor ice jams developed on small streams, but any flooding issues were very localized.||Behind the cold front, winds switched to a westerly direction and continued to be gusty throughout the day on Thursday, as colder temperatures moved into the region.  Additional wind gusts of 35 to 55 mph occurred during the daytime hours on Thursday, January 31st with some additional damage to trees and power lines.  Winds diminished by late Thursday afternoon as the pressure gradient began to relax over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread power outages occurred throughout Hamilton County as a result of high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Monday, March 18th, an area of low pressure moved towards the eastern Ohio Valley.  Precipitation well out ahead of the storm's warm front moved from southwest to northeast across the region.  With enough cold air in place, the precipitation fell in the form of snow during the evening hours. By just after midnight on Tuesday, March 19th, the steady precipitation ended or changed to patchy areas of freezing drizzle or sleet for areas from the Mohawk River southward. Meanwhile, steady snowfall continued across the Adirondacks and Lake George-Saratoga Region for the remainder of the overnight hours.||At the same time, another storm system began to develop along the mid-Atlantic coast.  This low pressure took a track just off the Northeast coast.  The track of this storm allowed some additional moisture off the Atlantic Ocean to be drawn into the region.  During the daylight hours on Tuesday, March 19th, an additional batch of steady snowfall developed across the entire region, as an upper level shortwave moved overhead.  While some additional snow accumulated, the strength of the March sun helped limit the amount of the accumulations, especially on paved surfaces.  Some additional snow showers and squalls lingered into the evening hours, before tapering off for Tuesday night.||At the end of the storm, snowfall amounts ranged from just 2 to 5 inches across parts of the mid-Hudson Valley and Taconics to 10 to 15 inches across the Sacandaga and Saratoga Regions. Most parts of the eastern Catskills and Capital Region received 5 to 9 inches of snow.
